tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for clinically significant tumor burden or progressive disease; also first-line for carcinoid syndrome symptom control

drugs.name: Octreotide acetate LAR
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 60 mg once a month) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses [162,165,166]. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID can be added.

biomarkers.requirement: Tumor likely to benefit from SSTR-directed therapy

tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 for nonfunctional GI NETs (with everolimus)

trial_data.os: Median OS 84.7 months octreotide vs 83.7 months placebo (HR 0.83; 95% CI 0.44-1.46; P=.51)
trial_data.pfs: Median TTP 14.3 months octreotide vs 6 months placebo (P=.000072)
trial_data.notes: Antiproliferative effect demonstrated [5,6]. CLARINET trial showed antiproliferative effect of lanreotide in advanced well-differentiated GEP NETs [6,7,8]. Post-study treatment included octreotide LAR in 38 of 43 patients in placebo arm.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks is recommended. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID can be considered. If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA. The effectiveness of everolimus in the treatment of patients with carcinoid syndrome has not been established [NE-G 1].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for clinically significant tumor burden or progressive disease; also first-line for carcinoid syndrome symptom control

drugs.name: Lanreotide
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 120 mg every 14 days) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses [162]. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID can be added.

biomarkers.requirement: Tumor likely to benefit from SSTR-directed therapy

trial_data.os: Not reported at final analysis
trial_data.pfs: PFS not reached vs 18 months (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.30-0.73; P<.001)
trial_data.notes: Lanreotide showed antiproliferative effect in advanced well-differentiated metastatic grade 1 and grade 2 GEP NETs [6]. Open-label extension estimated PFS at 32.8 months (95% CI 30.9-68.0) [161].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For symptom and/or tumor control, lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks is recommended. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA. The CLARINET FORTE phase 2 study demonstrated feasibility of reduced dosing interval (120 mg every 14 days) in patients with progressive disease [162].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locally advanced or metastatic disease
tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus or Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate

trial_data.os: Median OS 21.9 months cabozantinib vs 19.7 months placebo
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 8.4 months cabozantinib vs 3.9 months placebo
trial_data.or: 5% cabozantinib vs 0% placebo
trial_data.notes: Patients randomized 2:1 to cabozantinib or placebo. 62% of patients in cabozantinib arm had grade 3-5 adverse events possibly related to treatment, compared to 27% in placebo arm. Cabozantinib approved for PanNETs and extrapancreatic NETs in 2025 [1,177].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus or Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate. In the extrapancreatic NET cohort, stratified HR for progression or death was 0.38 [1]. Cabozantinib was approved for these indications in 2025.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locally advanced or metastatic disease

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 11.0 months everolimus vs 3.9 months placebo
trial_data.notes: Double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial. Randomized 2:1 to everolimus or placebo in patients with progressive, non-functional lung or GI NETs [3,173]. Patients were not receiving an SSA at the time of study enrollment. Drug-related grade 3/4 adverse events included stomatitis (9%), infections (7%), diarrhea (7%), anemia (4%), fatigue (3%), and hyperglycemia (3%) [173].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Everolimus is an inhibitor of mTOR. Effectiveness of everolimus in the treatment of patients with carcinoid syndrome has not been established [2,3]. RADIANT-2 trial randomized 429 patients with advanced NETs and carcinoid syndrome to everolimus/octreotide vs placebo/octreotide. PFS 16.4 months vs 11.3 months (P=.026) but did not meet predefined statistical significance threshold [2,168]. RADIANT-4 included 90 patients with lung primary. Exploratory analysis showed improved PFS for lung NET subgroup (HR 0.50; 95% CI 0.28-0.88) [3,173,175]. Adverse events include stomatitis, rash, fatigue, diarrhea, and rare pneumonitis [170-172].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (PRRT) - for GI NETs
tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive disease on octreotide LAR or lanreotide

drugs.name: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate
drugs.schedule: Every 8 weeks for a total of 4 treatments

biomarkers.requirement: Tumor uptake greater than normal liver on SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI

trial_data.os: Median OS 48.0 months lutetium Lu 177 dotatate vs 36.3 months high-dose octreotide (HR 0.84; P=.30)
trial_data.pfs: Not reached vs 8.4 months (P<.0001)
trial_data.or: 18% lutetium Lu 177 dotatate vs 3% high-dose octreotide (P<.001)
trial_data.notes: Randomized trial in advanced midgut NETs comparing lutetium Lu 177 dotatate plus octreotide LAR to high-dose octreotide LAR (60 mg every 4 weeks) [5,164,165]. Amino acid solution administered 30 minutes before, concurrently with, and 3 hours after lutetium Lu 177 dotatate for nephroprotection [NE-J].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Randomized data suggest superior outcomes in terms of PFS and response rates with PRRT compared to small molecules. Significant impact on overall survival is lacking [9,10]. Key eligibility: Well-differentiated NET; SSTR expression by NET; adequate bone marrow, renal, and hepatic function. PRRT should generally be avoided in patients with GFR <30 and used with caution in GFR 30-50 [NE-J]. Prior surgical evaluation should be performed before PRRT. Resection of primary tumors may reduce risk of PRRT-induced bowel obstruction [NE-J]. Short-term toxicities include nausea/vomiting related to amino acid infusion. Long-term risks include myelosuppression, secondary myelodysplastic syndrome and leukemia, renal toxicity, hepatic toxicity, and embryo-fetal toxicity [NE-J].

tier_1_regimens.site: Lung/Thymus well-differentiated NETs (atypical carcinoid with Ki-67/mitotic index in higher end of spectrum)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease

trial_data.os: Median OS 68 months (95% CI 35.3-100.7 months) in small retrospective study [200]; median OS 30.4 months in another study [201]
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 13 months (95% CI 4.4-21.6 months) in small retrospective study [200]; median PFS 9.0 months in another study [201]
trial_data.or: 30% in small retrospective study [200]
trial_data.notes: Capecitabine and temozolomide can be considered for intermediate-grade/atypical tumors with Ki-67 proliferative index and mitotic index in the higher end of the defined spectrum [198,202]. Temozolomide monotherapy showed PRs in 14% of patients in a retrospective study of 31 patients with progressive metastatic bronchial NETs [199].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. Temozolomide either administered alone or in combination with capecitabine is an option to manage tumor burden and any associated symptoms in patients with lung/thymic NETs [198,199].

tier_1_regimens.site: Lung/Thymus well-differentiated NETs (atypical carcinoid with Ki-67/mitotic index in higher end of spectrum)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease

trial_data.notes: Carboplatin/etoposide can be considered for intermediate-grade/atypical tumors with Ki-67 proliferative index and mitotic index in the higher end of the defined spectrum [198,202].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.site: Lung/Thymus well-differentiated NETs (atypical carcinoid with Ki-67/mitotic index in higher end of spectrum)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease

trial_data.notes: Cisplatin/etoposide can be considered for intermediate-grade/atypical tumors with Ki-67 proliferative index and mitotic index in the higher end of the defined spectrum [198,202,203].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease

trial_data.or: 14% PR in retrospective study of progressive metastatic bronchial NETs [199]
trial_data.notes: Temozolomide monotherapy was associated with PRs in 14% of patients in a retrospective study of 31 patients [199].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Temozolomide monotherapy can be considered for intermediate-grade/atypical tumors with Ki-67 proliferative index and mitotic index in the higher end of the defined spectrum [198,199]. Ekeblad et al. reported temozolomide as monotherapy was effective in treatment of advanced malignant neuroendocrine tumors [18].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Oxaliplatin-based therapy (eg, CAPEOX or FOLFOX) is listed as Other Recommended for lung/thymus NETs. For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, evidence of disease progression, or intermediate-grade disease (atypical carcinoid)

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 11.0 months everolimus vs 3.9 months placebo overall; lung subgroup HR 0.50 (95% CI 0.28-0.88) with 9.2 months vs 3.6 months
trial_data.notes: RADIANT-4 included 90 patients with lung primary (63 everolimus, 27 placebo). RADIANT-2 included 44 patients with lung NETs (including 9 atypical carcinoids); PFS HR 0.72 (95% CI 0.31-1.68, P=.228) [175,197]. Effectiveness of everolimus in carcinoid syndrome not established [2,3,175,197].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for nonfunctional lung NETs. Exploratory subgroup analysis from RADIANT-4 showed improved PFS by central review (HR 0.50; 95% CI 0.28-0.88) in the everolimus arm (9.2 months) compared to the placebo arm (3.6 months) for lung NETs [175]. Adverse events include stomatitis, rash, fatigue, and diarrhea [170-172].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, evidence of disease progression, intermediate-grade disease, or symptomatic disease
tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 8.4 months cabozantinib vs 3.9 months placebo overall; lung/thymic HR 0.17 (95% CI 0.07-0.42)
trial_data.notes: In the extrapancreatic NET cohort, lung/thymic NETs accounted for 33/134 patients in cabozantinib arm and 16/69 patients in placebo arm. Stratified HR for progression/death in lung/thymic subgroup was 0.17 [1].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus. Cabozantinib approved for extrapancreatic NETs in 2025 [1,177].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (PRRT) - for Lung/Thymic NETs
tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive disease on octreotide LAR or lanreotide, if SSTR-positive

drugs.name: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate
drugs.schedule: Every 8 weeks for a total of 4 treatments

biomarkers.requirement: Tumor uptake greater than normal liver on SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI

trial_data.os: Median OS 58.8 months (n=114) [205]; Median OS 63 months (95% CI 55-72 months) in Dutch cohort including bronchial NETs [204]
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 20.1 months typical bronchial carcinoid, 15.7 months atypical bronchial carcinoid (n=34) [206]; Median PFS 29 months (95% CI 26-33 months) in Dutch cohort [204]
trial_data.notes: Meta-analysis of 18 studies with 1920 patients found pooled disease response rate of 29%-31% and combined disease control rate of 74%-81% [194]. While prospective randomized studies are lacking for lung NETs, data from retrospective studies support efficacy [204,205,206].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Treatment with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate is a recommended option for patients with SSTR+ lung or thymic distant metastatic NETs with disease progression on octreotide LAR or lanreotide. A tumor is deemed SSTR-positive if the uptake in measurable lesions is greater than the liver [NE-J].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, evidence of disease progression, or symptomatic disease

drugs.name: Octreotide acetate LAR
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 60 mg once a month) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID can be added.

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 16.6 months (95% CI 11.3-21.9 months) for lanreotide; typical carcinoid PFS 21.9 months lanreotide vs 13.9 months placebo; atypical carcinoid 13.8 months vs 11.0 months
trial_data.or: 14% lanreotide vs 0% placebo (not statistically significant)
trial_data.notes: SPINET trial randomized 77 patients with bronchopulmonary NETs to lanreotide vs placebo [196]. Primary endpoint of centrally confirmed PFS showed no statistically significant difference. Patients were able to transition from double-blind to open-label phase to receive lanreotide.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients can be considered for treatment with octreotide LAR or lanreotide if the tumor is SSTR-positive and/or associated with hormonal symptoms [NE-G 2]. The Panel notes that no clear consensus exists on the timing of initiation in asymptomatic patients with metastatic NETs and low tumor burden.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Distant metastases with clinically significant tumor burden, evidence of disease progression, or symptomatic disease

drugs.name: Lanreotide
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 120 mg every 14 days) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses.

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 16.6 months (95% CI 11.3-21.9 months) overall; typical carcinoid 21.9 months vs 13.9 months placebo; atypical carcinoid 13.8 months vs 11.0 months
trial_data.or: 14% lanreotide vs 0% placebo (not statistically significant)
trial_data.notes: Phase III SPINET trial randomized 77 patients with bronchopulmonary NETs (typical and atypical carcinoids) to lanreotide plus best supportive care vs placebo plus best supportive care [196].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For symptom and/or tumor control, lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks is recommended. Higher doses have been shown to be safe.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease, if SSTR-positive

drugs.name: Octreotide acetate LAR
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 60 mg once a month) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID.

trial_data.pfs: PFS not reached vs 18 months (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.30-0.73; P<.001)
trial_data.notes: CLARINET included nonfunctioning pancreatic or intestinal NETs [6,160]. PROMID showed antiproliferative effect of octreotide LAR in advanced NETs of the midgut (TTP 14.3 vs 6 months; P=.000072) [5,158]. The CLARINET FORTE phase 2 study demonstrated feasibility of reduced dosing interval (lanreotide 120 mg every 14 days) in patients with PanNETs with progression on lanreotide (median PFS 5.6 months; 95% CI 5.5-8.3) [162].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with insulinoma, octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be used only if SSTR-based imaging is positive. In the absence of SSTRs, octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide can profoundly worsen hypoglycemia. Treatment with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide will likely be of greatest benefit in patients with SSTR-positive tumors [6,160]. After clinical, symptomatic, or radiographic progression on standard SSA doses, above-label dosing may be useful in select cases [162,165,166].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease, if SSTR-positive

drugs.name: Lanreotide
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses (up to 120 mg every 14 days) may be useful in select cases after progression on standard doses.

trial_data.pfs: PFS not reached vs 18 months (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.30-0.73; P<.001)
trial_data.notes: CLARINET FORTE showed lanreotide 120 mg every 14 days feasible in patients with progressive PanNETs (median PFS 5.6 months; 95% CI 5.5-8.3) [162].

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with insulinoma, lanreotide should be used only if SSTR-based imaging is positive [162,263].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease

trial_data.os: Median OS 44.0 months (95% CI 35.6-51.8 months) everolimus vs 37.7 months (95% CI 29.1-45.8 months) placebo (HR 0.94; 95% CI 0.73-1.20; P=.30)
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 11.0 months everolimus vs 4.6 months placebo (P<.001)
trial_data.notes: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase III trial [19,287,288]. Subset analysis suggested PFS benefit is independent of prior or concurrent SSA therapy or prior chemotherapy [289]. Adverse events include stomatitis, hyperglycemia, rash, diarrhea, and rare pneumonitis [170-172].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for progressive disease. Everolimus is an inhibitor of mTOR. Adverse events include stomatitis, hyperglycemia, rash, diarrhea, and rare pneumonitis [170-172,287,288].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease

trial_data.os: Median OS 38.6 months (95% CI 25.6-56.4 months) sunitinib vs 29.1 months (95% CI 16.4-36.8 months) placebo (HR 0.73; 95% CI 0.50-1.06; P=.094)
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 12.6 months (95% CI 11.1-20.6 months) sunitinib vs 5.8 months (95% CI 3.8-7.2 months) placebo
trial_data.notes: Multicenter randomized study by blinded independent central review [20,290]. No significant difference in OS; 69% of patients on placebo arm subsequently received sunitinib [20,291]. Long-term treatment (median 87.1 weeks) did not alter the safety profile [291]. Adverse events include fatigue, rare congestive heart failure, diarrhea, mucositis, and weakness [292,293].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for progressive disease. Sunitinib is an oral VEGF receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Adverse events include fatigue, rare congestive heart failure, diarrhea, mucositis, and weakness [290-293].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease
tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus, Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or Sunitinib

trial_data.os: Median OS 40.0 months cabozantinib vs 31.1 months placebo
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 13.8 months cabozantinib vs 4.4 months placebo
trial_data.or: 19% cabozantinib vs 0% placebo
trial_data.notes: Patients with PanNETs had to have had prior treatment with everolimus, sunitinib, or lutetium Lu 177 dotatate PRRT. 65% of patients in cabozantinib arm had grade 3/4 adverse event possibly related to treatment, compared to 23% in placebo arm [1,177]. Approved for PanNETs in 2025.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus, Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or Sunitinib. Approved for PanNETs in 2025 [1,177].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease; preferred when tumor response is needed for symptoms or cytoreduction

trial_data.os: Median OS 58.7 months temozolomide/capecitabine vs 53.8 months temozolomide alone (HR 0.82; P=.42)
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 22.7 months temozolomide/capecitabine vs 14.4 months temozolomide alone (HR 0.58; P=.022)
trial_data.notes: Randomized phase II study [21,299]. Temozolomide-based therapy is the most commonly used chemotherapy regimen for PanNET. Temozolomide with or without capecitabine may have more activity in tumors arising in the pancreas compared to GI NETs [295,298].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred when tumor response is needed for symptoms or cytoreduction. Temozolomide with or without capecitabine may have more activity in tumors arising in the pancreas compared to GI NETs [21,295-299]. For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

trial_data.os: ORR 45.2% in aggressive PanNETs after prior capecitabine/temozolomide; disease control rate 93.5%; median PFS 6 months (95% CI 5.0-7.0 months); median OS 16 months (95% CI 11.3-20.7 months) from start of study, 67 months (95% CI 49.8-84.2 months) from time of diagnosis [304]
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 9 months and OS 30 months in study with 89 PanNET patients [180]; Median PFS 12.6 months and OS 29.4 months in study with 33 PanNET patients [303]
trial_data.or: 45.2% in aggressive PanNETs [304]; PR rate 30% in 89 PanNET patients [180]; disease control rate 83.3% [303]
trial_data.notes: Other cytotoxic agents also have activity in PanNETs [180,302,303,304].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Listed as Other Recommended for PanNETs. For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease with bulky, symptomatic, and/or progressive disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: Listed as Other Recommended for PanNETs. For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.site: GI tract well-differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs (carcinoid syndrome)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Refractory diarrhea due to carcinoid syndrome

trial_data.notes: Multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase III trial [220]. Mean BM frequency reductions per day from baseline to week 12: placebo -0.9, telotristat 250 mg -1.7, telotristat 500 mg -2.1. Both telotristat dosages significantly decreased mean urinary 5-HIAA compared to placebo at week 12 (P<.001) [220]. Treatment-related adverse events reported in 45.2% of patients in long-term TELEPATH study [223].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Telotristat ethyl is a small-molecule tryptophan hydroxylase (TPH) inhibitor, which decreases urinary 5-HIAA levels and frequency of bowel movements in patients with carcinoid syndrome. Patients should be evaluated for pancreatic exocrine deficiency and bile acid diarrhea. Symptomatic benefit can sometimes be delayed for several weeks after initiation. Compared to placebo, treatment with telotristat did not result in a statistically significant change in the number of observed flushing episodes [220-223].

trial_data.os: OS data not mature at time of analysis
trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 22.8 months (95% CI 19.4 months-not estimated) lutetium Lu 177 dotatate vs 8.5 months (95% CI 7.7-13.8 months) control arm
trial_data.ci: 95% CI 19.4 months-not estimated; 95% CI 7.7-13.8 months control
trial_data.notes: Enrolled patients with advanced high-risk SSTR+ GEP-NET (Ki-67 index 10%-55%) in the first-line setting [4,166]. Median Ki-67 index was 16% (range 12%-25%), 65% had G2 disease, 46% had GI primary, 54% had PanNET. Consistent benefit across all subgroups including G2 vs G3 and pancreas vs non-pancreas vs small intestine primary [166]. One case of myelodysplasia was noted in lutetium Lu 177 dotatate plus octreotide LAR group.

tier_1_regimens.notes: First-line PRRT (typically along with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide) can be considered in patients with advanced GEP-NETs with Ki-67 10%-55% [NE-J]. In the absence of a clear survival benefit and long-term safety data, the use of lutetium Lu 177 dotatate plus octreotide LAR or lanreotide in the first-line setting should be individualized.

drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID.

drugs.name: Lanreotide
drugs.schedule: Every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, short-acting octreotide 100-250 mcg SC TID.

trial_data.notes: Lanreotide arm required less frequent short-acting octreotide (33.7% vs 48.5%; P=.017) [216]. Improvement in symptom control irrespective of prior octreotide use [216,217].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Therapeutic levels of octreotide would not be expected to be reached for 10-14 days after LAR injection. Short-acting SC octreotide PRN can help address suboptimal symptom control quickly. For patients with carcinoid syndrome, an echocardiogram should be performed every 1-3 years or as clinically indicated for patients without CHD, and at least annually for patients with established CHD [NET-14,225-227].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Preoperative/symptom management
tier_1_regimens.notes: For insulinomas, stabilize glucose levels with diet (complex carbohydrates) and/or diazoxide or everolimus [264-266,285]. The hyperglycemia associated with everolimus can be leveraged to stabilize glucose levels in patients with insulinomas [285]. Octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide can be considered but only if tumor expresses SSTRs. In the absence of SSTRs, octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide can profoundly worsen hypoglycemia [263,283,284].

trial_data.n_patients: 22 patients with PanNET (61 total in study)
trial_data.or: 91% in 22 patients with PanNET; 14% achieved CR (follow-up report after median 37.8 months confirmed 91% ORR, CR in 7/22 [32%]) [261,262]
trial_data.notes: Median time to response 5.5 months (range 2.5-16.4 months), median duration of response not reached [261]. Decision to use belzutifan in small resectable tumors needs to be individualized. Data for use for larger tumors, locally advanced, and distant disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials ongoing (NCT04924075). Most common grade 3 adverse events were anemia (8%), hypertension (8%), and fatigue (5%) [262].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Belzutifan is an oral HIF2α inhibitor approved in 2021 for VHL disease-associated tumors including PanNETs [261,262]. Data for the use of belzutifan for larger tumors, locally advanced unresectable tumors, and distant disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials are ongoing (NCT04924075).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Locoregional advanced and/or distant metastatic disease

drugs.name: Systemic therapy based on tumor type (excluding PRRT)
drugs.dose: Per regimen specifications based on tumor type

biomarkers.requirement: Insufficient uptake on SSTR-based imaging (lower than liver, Krenning score 0-2)

tier_1_regimens.notes: SSTR-negative refers to tumors that show insufficient uptake on SSTR-based imaging (lower than liver, Krenning score 0-2). SSTR-negative disease and mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease is more common with higher grade (G3) disease and atypical lung carcinoid. Consider use of SSAs in patients with SSTR-negative disease and non-functional tumors. Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3-6 months) is recommended for patients with SSTR-negative disease who are treated with SSAs [SSTRNEG-1,286]. PRRT can be considered for mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease but might not work as well as for homogeneous SSTR-positive disease.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (above-label dosing)
tier_1_regimens.site: GI tract, Lung/Thymus, and Pancreas well-differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs
tier_1_regimens.setting: Disease progression on standard SSA doses, SSTR-positive

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 8.3 months (95% CI 5.6-11.1) midgut NETs with lanreotide 120 mg every 14 days; Median PFS 5.6 months (95% CI 5.5-8.3) PanNETs with lanreotide 120 mg every 14 days [162]
trial_data.notes: NETTER-1 and NETTER-2 control arms used octreotide LAR 60 mg/month [164-166]. Above-label dosing is feasible and may provide benefit [162,165,166].

tier_1_regimens.notes: After clinical, symptomatic, or radiographic progression on standard SSA doses, for symptom and/or tumor control, above-label dosing octreotide LAR (up to 60 mg once a month) or lanreotide (up to 120 mg every 14 days) may be useful in select cases [162,165,166]. The Panel notes that most studies assessing high-dose SSAs have focused on patients with low-risk tumors [162,164,165].

tier_2_handbook.definition_Gastric_NETs: Gastric neuroendocrine tumors (gNETs) are well-differentiated neuroendocrine neoplasms arising from enterochromaffin-like (ECL) cells of the gastric mucosa. They are classified into distinct subtypes based on underlying etiology, histological features, and clinical behavior. Type 1 gNETs occur in the context of chronic atrophic gastritis (autoimmune or H. pylori-related) leading to hypergastrinemia and ECL-cell hyperplasia; these are typically multifocal, small (<1 cm), and have a low metastatic risk (<5%). Type 2 gNETs are associated with hypergastrinemia from gastrinomas (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome), often in patients with multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), and are also multifocal but carry a higher metastatic potential. Type 3 gNETs are sporadic, unifocal, and arise in normal gastric mucosa with normal gastrin levels; these are more aggressive with a higher propensity for metastasis. A fourth category, often termed 'other' or PPI-induced gNETs, is linked to long-term proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use, which causes hypergastrinemia and ECL-cell hyperplasia; these tumors appear to have a much lower propensity to metastasize compared to sporadic type 3 tumors. Gastric NETs are part of the broader group of gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (GEP-NETs) and are typically slow-growing, but accurate subtyping is crucial for prognosis and management. The diagnosis requires endoscopic evaluation with biopsy and assessment of gastrin levels, and staging is based on tumor size, depth of invasion, and lymph node status. [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026, NET-1, Discussion MS-10, MS-11]
tier_2_handbook.subtypes_Gastric_NETs: Gastric NETs are subdivided into four main categories based on clinical and pathogenetic features: (1) Type 1 (Hypergastrinemic/Atrophic Gastritis-associated): These are the most common (70-80% of cases), arising in patients with chronic atrophic gastritis, often autoimmune, leading to achlorhydria and elevated gastrin levels. They are typically multifocal, small (<1 cm), and confined to the gastric body/fundus, with a very low metastatic risk (<5%). Histologically, they show ECL-cell hyperplasia in the background mucosa. (2) Type 2 (Hypergastrinemic/Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome-associated): Rare (5-10% of cases), associated with gastrinomas, often in the context of MEN1 syndrome. They are also multifocal and have a higher metastatic potential than type 1. Background mucosa shows parietal cell hyperplasia and ECL-cell hyperplasia. (3) Type 3 (Sporadic): Unifocal, sporadic tumors that arise in normal gastric mucosa without hypergastrinemia. They are more aggressive, with a significant risk of lymph node and distant metastases, and often present as larger lesions (>2 cm). (4) PPI-induced (or 'other'): Associated with long-term proton pump inhibitor use, which induces hypergastrinemia and ECL-cell hyperplasia. These tumors are biologically indolent, with a lower metastatic potential than sporadic type 3 tumors, but data on their behavior are emerging. Less common subtypes include somatostatinomas and gastrinomas primary to the stomach. The 2022 WHO classification recommends grading based on mitotic rate and Ki-67 index: Grade 1 (G1, Ki-67 <3%), Grade 2 (G2, Ki-67 3-20%), and Grade 3 (G3, Ki-67 >20%). [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026, NET-1, NE-E 4 of 9, Discussion MS-10, MS-11]
tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis_Gastric_NETs: The molecular pathogenesis of gastric NETs varies by subtype and is not fully elucidated. Type 1 gNETs arise due to chronic hypergastrinemia secondary to autoimmune or H. pylori-induced atrophic gastritis, leading to loss of acid feedback and ECL-cell hyperplasia, which can progress to dysplasia and neoplasia. This process is driven by gastrin trophic effects, but specific driver mutations are not well-defined. Type 2 gNETs are associated with gastrinomas, often in MEN1 syndrome, where inactivating mutations in the MEN1 gene (encoding menin) on chromosome 11q13 lead to loss of tumor suppression, resulting in gastrin-secreting tumors and subsequent ECL-cell hyperplasia. Sporadic type 3 gNETs may involve mutations in genes such as CDKN1B (encoding p27, a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor), which has been identified in small bowel carcinoid tumors and could contribute to uncontrolled cell growth. Other somatic mutations reported in sporadic pancreatic NETs (e.g., in DAXX, ATRX, and mTOR pathway genes like PIK3CA, PTEN) may also play a role, but specific data for gastric NETs are limited. PPI-induced gNETs likely result from sustained hypergastrinemia due to acid suppression, but their indolent nature suggests additional molecular differences from sporadic tumors. Epigenetic changes, such as DNA methylation, and alterations in signaling pathways (e.g., Wnt, Notch) are areas of ongoing research. Immunohistochemical markers like synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and INSM1 confirm neuroendocrine differentiation, but they are not predictive of pathogenesis. Overall, the molecular landscape is heterogeneous, and further studies are needed to clarify driver events in each subtype. [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026, Discussion MS-7, MS-51, NE-E 1 of 9, References 10, 91]
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology_Gastric_NETs: Epidemiological data for gastric NETs are limited compared to other GEP-NETs, but available studies indicate distinct patterns by subtype. Type 1 gNETs are the most common, accounting for 70-80% of cases, and are frequently diagnosed in patients with pernicious anemia or autoimmune gastritis, with a higher prevalence in females and older adults. The incidence has increased over time, likely due to improved endoscopic detection. Type 2 gNETs are rare (5-10% of cases) and occur almost exclusively in patients with MEN1 syndrome or sporadic gastrinomas. Type 3 gNETs represent 15-25% of cases and are sporadic, with no clear demographic predilection, but they tend to present at a more advanced stage. A SEER database analysis of 3,523 patients with gastric neuroendocrine neoplasms reported a 5-year overall survival rate of 53.7%, with significant variation by subtype: type 1 and 2 tumors have excellent survival (>90% at 5 years), while type 3 tumors have a poorer prognosis (5-year survival ~50%). The annual incidence of all gastric NETs in the United States is approximately 0.3 per 100,000 persons, based on SEER data from 2000-2016. There is a slight female predominance, and most cases are diagnosed between ages 50 and 70. Geographic variations exist, with higher reported incidences in North America and Europe, possibly due to better diagnostics. PPI-induced gNETs are increasingly recognized, but epidemiological data are sparse. Overall, gastric NETs are rare, but their incidence is rising, underscoring the importance of awareness and accurate subtyping. [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026, Discussion MS-10, Reference 5, 128, SEER data]
tier_2_handbook.risk_factors_Gastric_NETs: Risk factors for gastric NETs are primarily linked to the underlying etiology of each subtype. For type 1 gNETs, the main risk factors include chronic atrophic gastritis (autoimmune or H. pylori-related), which leads to hypergastrinemia and ECL-cell hyperplasia. Conditions such as pernicious anemia, autoimmune disorders, and H. pylori infection are associated with increased risk. For type 2 gNETs, the presence of gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome) is the key risk factor, often in the context of MEN1 syndrome, which has a strong hereditary component. Type 3 gNETs are sporadic, and no specific environmental risk factors are well-established, though a family history of NETs or other neuroendocrine neoplasias may increase susceptibility. PPI-induced gNETs are associated with long-term use of proton pump inhibitors (typically >1 year), which causes hypergastrinemia and ECL-cell stimulation; however, the risk appears low and depends on the duration and dosage of PPI therapy. Other potential risk factors mentioned in general NET literature include obesity, diabetes, and smoking, but these are not specifically validated for gastric NETs. Genetic predispositions include germline mutations in MEN1 (for type 2 and some sporadic cases), and possibly other genes like SDHx, though data are limited. The strength of evidence varies: for chronic atrophic gastritis and PPI use, evidence is moderate based on observational studies; for MEN1 and gastrinomas, evidence is strong due to well-defined syndromes; for sporadic risk factors, evidence is weak and largely based on case-control studies. [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026, NET-1, Discussion MS-10, MS-11, References 132, 133]
tier_2_handbook.protective_factors_Gastric_NETs: No specific protective factors for gastric NETs are explicitly mentioned in the source text. The risk is primarily driven by pathological conditions (e.g., chronic gastritis, gastrinoma) or iatrogenic factors (e.g., PPI use), rather than modifiable protective factors. General measures that might reduce risk include early detection and management of H. pylori infection to prevent atrophic gastritis, but this is not directly stated as protective. Similarly, judicious use of PPIs may mitigate the risk of PPI-induced gNETs, but evidence is limited. Overall, the source does not identify any evidence-based protective factors. [References: NCCN Guidelines v1.2026]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) are a broad family of neoplasms arising from the diffuse endocrine system, most commonly originating in the gastrointestinal tract, lungs/bronchi, thymus, and pancreas [1,2]. Patients may present with symptoms of hormone hypersecretion (functional tumors) or with mass-effect symptoms (nonfunctional tumors). Approximately 8% to 28% of patients with GI NETs develop carcinoid syndrome [102,103], characterized by flushing, diarrhea, bronchoconstriction, and cardiac valvular fibrosis. Pancreatic NETs (PanNETs) include functioning tumors such as gastrinomas (peptic ulcers), insulinomas (fasting hypoglycemia), glucagonomas (necrolytic migratory erythema), and VIPomas (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria). Nonfunctional PanNETs account for approximately 60% to 91% of cases [14,73]. Pheochromocytomas (PCCs) and paragangliomas (PGLs) present with hypertension, tachycardia, sweating, and paroxysmal headache. Adrenocortical carcinomas (ACCs) present with symptoms of hormone excess in approximately 60% of patients or with tumor-related symptoms such as abdominal pain and weight loss [21].

symptoms.name: Flushing
symptoms.detail: Episodic cutaneous vasodilation, predominantly in the face and upper trunk, typically occurring in the context of carcinoid syndrome. Serotonin, histamine, tachykinins, and other vasoactive substances contribute to flushing. Etiology of flushing is less well understood than that of diarrhea [12,100].
symptoms.frequency: Common in carcinoid syndrome (8%–28% of all GI NETs); rarely seen without liver metastases unless retroperitoneal disease is present

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.detail: Carcinoid syndrome–associated diarrhea is caused by serotonin and other vasoactive peptides. Diarrhea is multifactorial in NET patients and may also be caused by direct side effects from somatostatin analogs (SSAs), pancreatic exocrine insufficiency resulting in steatorrhea, bile malabsorption from ileocecectomy or cholecystectomy, and short-gut syndrome [NE-K].
symptoms.frequency: Common in carcinoid syndrome; also seen in VIPomas (profuse watery diarrhea with electrolyte depletion), glucagonomas, and somatostatinomas (diarrhea/steatorrhea)

symptoms.name: Peptic ulcer disease
symptoms.detail: Gastrinomas secrete gastrin, causing recurrent peptic ulcers, dyspepsia, and diarrhea. Approximately 70% of patients with MEN1 and gastrinoma have tumors in the duodenum [244]. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome results from gastrin hypersecretion.
symptoms.frequency: Characteristic of gastrinomas; associated with gastrinoma (10% of functioning PanNETs) [232]

symptoms.name: Hypoglycemia
symptoms.detail: Insulinomas secrete insulin resulting in fasting or nocturnal hypoglycemia. Approximately 70% to 90% of sporadic insulinomas are benign and can be cured surgically. Evaluation with a 72-hour fast tests serum insulin, pro-insulin, and C-peptide during concurrent hypoglycemia [245].
symptoms.frequency: Characteristic of insulinomas (≤70% of functioning PanNETs; only 10% associated with metastases) [232]

symptoms.name: Necrolytic migratory erythema
symptoms.detail: Characteristic dermatitis of glucagonomas, often accompanied by hyperglycemia or diabetes mellitus, weight loss, cachexia, and a hypercoagulable state (10%–33% risk of thromboembolic events) [270,271].
symptoms.frequency: Characteristic of glucagonomas (~15% of functioning PanNETs) [232]

symptoms.name: Watery diarrhea with hypokalemia and achlorhydria (WDHA syndrome)
symptoms.detail: VIPomas secrete vasoactive intestinal peptide causing severe secretory diarrhea with profound electrolyte depletion and dehydration. Also known as Verner-Morrison syndrome.

symptoms.name: Cushing syndrome
symptoms.detail: Ectopic adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) production by bronchial or thymic NETs can cause Cushing syndrome. Bronchial and thymic NETs have been associated with ectopic ACTH production [98,99]. Also may occur from adrenal adenoma, adrenal carcinoma, pituitary adenoma, or ectopic sources (lung or thymic NETs). Clinical features include central weight gain, striae, hypertension, hyperglycemia, depression, hirsutism, and muscle weakness [NE-A 2 of 4].
symptoms.frequency: Uncommon; seen in bronchial and thymic NETs, and adrenal cortical carcinoma

symptoms.name: Acromegaly
symptoms.detail: GH-secreting pituitary adenomas in MEN1 syndrome, or rarely ectopic GH secretion by other NETs.

symptoms.name: Hypertension (paroxysmal or sustained)
symptoms.detail: PCCs release catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) and their metabolites metanephrine and normetanephrine, resulting in hypertension, arrhythmia, and/or hyperglycemia. Symptoms include sweating, paroxysmal headache, pallor episodes, and syncope (very rare) [NE-A 2 of 4]. PGLs secrete catecholamines in about 40% of cases. Head and neck PGLs secrete catecholamines only about 5% of the time.
symptoms.frequency: Characteristic of PCCs/PGLs; combined annual incidence in the United States estimated at 500–1600 cases [17]

symptoms.name: Virilization/Feminization
symptoms.detail: Androgen-secreting ACCs in females may induce hirsutism, virilization, deepening of the voice, and oligo/amenorrhea. In males, estrogen-secreting tumors may induce gynecomastia and testicular atrophy. Approximately 60% of ACC patients present with evidence of adrenal steroid hormone excess [21].
symptoms.frequency: Seen in functioning adrenocortical carcinomas (~60% of ACCs) [21]

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain / mass effect
symptoms.detail: Nonfunctioning tumors may present with symptoms related to local growth including abdominal pain, back pain, early satiety, and weight loss. Small bowel NETs often present with intermittent abdominal pain from episodic bowel obstruction or bowel ischemia related to the primary tumor and surrounding fibrosis.
symptoms.frequency: Common; nonfunctioning PanNETs account for 60%–91% of cases [14,73]

symptoms.name: Respiratory symptoms
symptoms.detail: Lung NETs may present with cough, hemoptysis, wheezing, recurrent pneumonia, or incidental pulmonary nodules. Diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia (DIPNECH) presents with chronic cough and dyspnea.
symptoms.frequency: NETs of the lung or bronchus account for 30.6% of all NETs [123]

symptoms.name: Hypercalcemia
symptoms.detail: Primary hyperparathyroidism associated with MEN1 or MEN2, characterized by elevated serum calcium. The most common manifestation of MEN1 is parathyroid adenoma/hyperplasia (present in >98% of patients) [10,494].
symptoms.frequency: Very common in MEN1 (>98%); ~25% of MEN2A

symptoms.name: Visual field defects / headache
symptoms.detail: Pituitary adenomas in MEN1 may present with headaches, visual field deficits, galactorrhea (prolactinoma), or amenorrhea. About 30%–40% of MEN1 patients have functioning pituitary tumors [10,493].

signs.name: Flushing
signs.detail: Episodic erythema of the face and upper trunk, a hallmark of carcinoid syndrome. May occur with diarrhea and bronchospasm.

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: Palpable liver from metastatic disease, commonly seen in midgut NETs with hepatic metastases. The liver is the most common metastatic site for NETs.

signs.name: Cardiac murmur (tricuspid regurgitation/pulmonary stenosis)
signs.detail: Carcinoid heart disease (CHD) causes valvular fibrosis, predominantly affecting the tricuspid and pulmonary valves. Approximately 50% to 66% of patients with carcinoid syndrome develop valvular cardiac complications [101]. Echocardiographic diagnosis should include morphologic evaluation of valves and assessment of right heart size and function [NE-B 2 of 5].
signs.frequency: 50%–66% of patients with carcinoid syndrome; 59% had tricuspid regurgitation in one study [225,226]

signs.name: Peripheral edema / ascites
signs.detail: Signs of right-sided heart failure from carcinoid heart disease or from portal hypertension/hepatic insufficiency related to extensive liver metastases.
signs.frequency: Seen in advanced carcinoid syndrome and metastatic disease

signs.name: Central obesity, striae, hypertension
signs.detail: Clinical features of hypercortisolemia (Cushing syndrome), which may be caused by ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, adrenal cortical tumor, or ectopic ACTH from bronchial/thymic NETs. Also includes proximal muscle weakness, hyperglycemia, depression, and hirsutism [NE-A 2 of 4].
signs.frequency: Seen in functioning ACC (~60%) and ACTH-secreting tumors

signs.name: Hirsutism / virilization
signs.detail: Androgen-secreting adrenal cortical carcinoma causing hirsutism, virilization, deepening voice, and menstrual irregularities in females.

signs.name: Gynecomastia
signs.detail: Estrogen-secreting adrenal cortical carcinoma or testicular atrophy.

signs.name: Pallor, diaphoresis, tachycardia
signs.detail: During paroxysmal hypertensive episodes in pheochromocytoma, patients may exhibit pallor, profuse sweating, and tachycardia. Syncope is very rare [NE-A 2 of 4].

signs.name: Abdominal mass
signs.detail: Large nonfunctioning PanNETs or ACC may be palpable on abdominal examination.
signs.frequency: Seen in large tumors; ACC often presents at a larger size than benign adenomas

signs.name: Mucosal neuromas / Marfanoid habitus
signs.detail: Clinical features of MEN2B, including mucosal neuromas of the lips and tongue, medullated corneal nerve fibers, distinctive facies, Marfanoid body habitus, and poor dentition. Nearly all patients with MEN2B have Marfanoid habitus [232].

signs.name: Cafe-au-lait spots / neurofibromas
signs.detail: Features of neurofibromatosis type 1, which is associated with an increased risk of PCC (~3%) and rare PanNETs.

atypical_or_missed: Carcinoid syndrome may take years to develop, typically only manifesting after liver metastases are present; earlier, nonspecific symptoms like mild flushing or intermittent diarrhea may be dismissed
Small bowel NETs present with intermittent, vague abdominal pain that may be misdiagnosed as irritable bowel syndrome; diagnosis is often delayed

Gastrinoma-related peptic ulcer disease may be misattributed to H. pylori or NSAID use if serum gastrin is not measured; most patients with elevated gastrin do not have gastrinoma but rather achlorhydria or PPI use [244]
Insulinoma symptoms (confusion, diaphoresis, visual changes) may be attributed to psychiatric illness, neurologic conditions, or other causes of hypoglycemia
Glucagonoma-associated necrolytic migratory erythema may be misdiagnosed as dermatitis, eczema, or other dermatologic conditions before hormonal diagnosis
VIPoma-associated chronic diarrhea may be misdiagnosed as chronic idiopathic diarrhea or functional bowel disease
Pheochromocytoma may go undetected until perioperative hypertensive crisis; patients with apparently sporadic PCCs should be evaluated before any surgical procedure or invasive intervention
Adrenocortical carcinoma presenting with subtle virilization may be misdiagnosed as PCOS or idiopathic hirsutism
Incidentally discovered pancreatic NETs may be undertreated due to lack of awareness of malignant potential, even in small tumors
Lung NETs may be misdiagnosed as asthma, COPD, or recurrent pneumonia, delaying diagnosis
Rectal NETs discovered incidentally as polyps during colonoscopy may be misclassified as benign polyps without appropriate staging
Type 1 gastric NETs may not be recognized without specific testing for atrophic gastritis and hypergastrinemia, as they are often multifocal and small
DIPNECH presenting with chronic cough and dyspnea may be confused with obstructive airway disease
Somatostatinomas causing diabetes, cholelithiasis, and steatorrhea may be attributed to more common conditions before the tumor is identified

red_flags: Carcinoid crisis: life-threatening massive release of vasoactive substances triggered by tumor manipulation or anesthesia, with severe hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia, arrhythmias), diarrhea, and bronchoconstriction; prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis [NE-K]; intraoperative octreotide should not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration

Paroxysmal hypertensive crisis with headache, diaphoresis, and tachycardia: suggests pheochromocytoma and requires evaluation with plasma free metanephrines before any surgical procedure or invasive intervention

Refractory peptic ulcer disease with diarrhea: suggests gastrinoma/Zollinger-Ellison syndrome and requires serum gastrin measurement; PPI use should ideally be paused to confirm diagnosis but continued if patient has overt symptoms or risk of complications

Hypoglycemia with neuroglycopenic symptoms: suggests insulinoma and requires 72-hour supervised fast with concurrent measurement of insulin, pro-insulin, and C-peptide; rule out other causes of hypoglycemia first
Severe secretory diarrhea with hypokalemia and achlorhydria: suggests VIPoma (Verner-Morrison syndrome)
Necrolytic migratory erythema with diabetes and weight loss: suggests glucagonoma; evaluate for hypercoagulable state (10%–33% risk) [270,271]
New-onset Cushing syndrome features: requires evaluation for ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, adrenal tumor, or ectopic ACTH source including bronchial/thymic NETs [NE-A 2 of 4]
Sustained or paroxysmal hypertension in a young patient (<45 years) or with family history of endocrine tumors: suggests hereditary PCC/PGL syndrome requiring genetic counseling and testing [19,448]

Adrenal mass >4 cm or with irregular margins, heterogeneity, or invasion: suspicion for adrenocortical carcinoma requiring staging workup with FDG-PET/CT, chest CT, and adrenal protocol CT or MRI [21]
Symptoms of carcinoid heart disease: dyspnea, fatigue, edema, or ascites in patients with known carcinoid syndrome; echocardiography required

Hereditary syndrome red flags: presence of 2+ MEN1-associated tumors (parathyroid hyperplasia, enteropancreatic NETs, pituitary tumors), MTC with or without PCC (MEN2), bilateral PCC, family history of endocrine tumors, or known pathogenic germline variant

Perioperative hemodynamic instability during surgery for suspected carcinoid syndrome: requires immediate treatment with vasopressors and fluid resuscitation; do not delay for octreotide administration

diagnostic.test: Plasma free or 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines
diagnostic.rationale: Primary diagnostic test for pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma. Elevations 3 times above the upper limit of normal are diagnostic [NE-A 2 of 4]. For cervical PGL, measurement of serum/24-hour urine fractionated catecholamines (for dopamine) or methoxytyramine may be appropriate.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated metanephrines or normetanephrines suggest PCC or PGL. Both catecholamines and metanephrines/normetanephrines can produce false-positive results. Concurrent medications should be reviewed for interference (acetaminophen, labetalol, tricyclic antidepressants, MAOIs, sympathomimetics, cocaine, sulfasalazine, levodopa) [NE-A 2 of 4].

diagnostic.rationale: Diagnostic for gastrinoma/Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Must be measured fasting and ideally off PPI therapy for >1 week, though PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms or complication risk [244, NE-A 3 of 4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated fasting gastrin level. Most patients with elevated gastrin do not have gastrinoma but have achlorhydria or PPI use. Elevated gastrin levels are usually suggestive of type 1 or type 2 gNETs [NET-1 footnotes].

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation of serotonin secretion for carcinoid syndrome diagnosis. Recommended in patients with symptoms consistent with carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhea) [NE-A 1 of 4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated 5-HIAA levels. Patients should avoid avocados, bananas, cantaloupe, eggplant, pineapples, plums, tomatoes, hickory nuts/pecans, plantains, kiwi, dates, grapefruit, honeydew, and walnuts for 48 hours prior to and during testing [NE-A 1 of 4]. Medications that can increase 5-HIAA include acetaminophen, ephedrine, diazepam, nicotine, glyceryl guaiacolate, and phenobarbital.

diagnostic.test: Fasting blood glucose, insulin, pro-insulin, C-peptide
diagnostic.rationale: Diagnostic workup for suspected insulinoma during concurrent hypoglycemia. Gold standard is 72-hour supervised fast [245].
diagnostic.key_findings: Insulin >3 mcIU/mL (usually >6 mcIU/mL), C-peptide ≥0.6 ng/mL, proinsulin ≥5 pmol/L when fasting blood glucose is <55 mg/dL is suspicious for insulinoma [245]. Rule out other causes of hypoglycemia first.

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for suspected glucagonoma in patients with recent-onset diabetes, cachexia, and/or necrolytic erythematous skin rash.
Evaluation for suspected VIPoma presenting with watery diarrhea.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated VIP level; hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, metabolic acidosis (reduced HCO3-).

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for suspected somatostatinoma presenting with hyperglycemia, cholelithiasis, and diarrhea/steatorrhea.

Often elevated in patients with NETs (>60% of patients with functioning or nonfunctioning PanNETs) [238-240]. Elevated levels to twice the normal limit or higher are associated with shorter survival times (HR, 2.8; 95% CI, 1.9–4.0; P < .001) [241]. However, should not be relied upon in isolation due to false elevations from PPIs, renal or liver failure, hypertension, and chronic gastritis [115-117].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated chromogranin A. Levels elevated ≥2× upper limit of normal associated with worse prognosis.

diagnostic.test: Plasma aldosterone and plasma renin activity
diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for primary aldosteronism. Screening test for suspected adrenal cortical aldosterone-producing adenoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: Suppressed renin/renin activity in association with elevated plasma aldosterone (>10 ng/dL) suggests primary aldosteronism. Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio usually >30 in primary aldosteronism [409]. Confirmatory testing: oral or IV salt loading and measurement of urine or plasma aldosterone to test for adequate suppression [413].

diagnostic.test: Overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test / 2-3 midnight salivary cortisols / 24-hour urinary free cortisol
diagnostic.rationale: Screening for hypercortisolemia (± Cushing syndrome) in patients with symptoms suggesting cortisol excess [NE-A 2 of 4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Failure to suppress cortisol after overnight dexamethasone, elevated midnight salivary cortisol, or elevated 24-hour urinary free cortisol indicates hypercortisolemia. If confirmed, plasma ACTH should be measured in the morning to determine if ACTH-dependent or independent.

diagnostic.rationale: To determine if hypercortisolemia is ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic source) or ACTH-independent (adrenal source) [NE-A 2 of 4]. ACTH <5 pg/mL suggests ACTH-independent disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Low ACTH with elevated cortisol suggests adrenal source. Elevated ACTH with elevated cortisol suggests pituitary or ectopic source.

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for sex steroid excess in suspected adrenocortical carcinoma. ACC can secrete multiple hormones [NE-A 2 of 4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated adrenal androgens suggest functioning ACC. ACCs often secrete multiple hormones.

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for pituitary adenomas in MEN1: serum IGF-1 (category 2B), serum prolactin, LH/FSH, gonadotrophin with end organ hormone, alpha subunits (mainly in non-secretory tumors), TSH and free T4, plasma ACTH/AM cortisol, total and free testosterone, estradiol [NE-A 2 of 4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated prolactin indicates prolactinoma. Elevated IGF-1 indicates acromegaly.

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluation for medullary thyroid carcinoma in MEN2. Basal calcitonin and CEA levels guide extent of nodal dissection required.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated calcitonin and/or CEA suggests MTC. Low calcitonin with high CEA levels usually have more aggressive tumors.

diagnostic.rationale: Annual screening for hyperparathyroidism in MEN1. If calcium rises, further workup needed.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated calcium (corrected for albumin) indicates hypercalcemia. Further studies should be done when serum calcium is ≥10 mg/dL since normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism is an early sign of MEN1 [MEN1-1 footnote c].

diagnostic.rationale: If calcium is elevated, PTH measurement confirms primary hyperparathyroidism.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated PTH with hypercalcemia confirms primary hyperparathyroidism.

diagnostic.test: Molecular profiling of tumor tissue (NGS panel)
diagnostic.rationale: Recommended for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Testing should specifically consider NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E mutations, MSI-H, dMMR, and TMB-H [PDNEC-1 footnote g]. For PDNEC, testing on tumor tissue is preferred; ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of actionable mutations including NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E, MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb by FDA-approved test).

diagnostic.test: Genetic counseling and germline genetic testing
diagnostic.rationale: Recommended for patients meeting criteria in Principles of Hereditary Cancer Risk Assessment (NE-F). Mandatory for ACC, PCC/PGL, MEN1/MEN2 features, multifocal PanNETs, gastrinomas, thymic NETs, and others [NE-F 1 of 6].
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of germline pathogenic variants in MEN1, RET, VHL, SDHx, NF1, CDKN1B, and other genes.

staging.test: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast
staging.rationale: Primary cross-sectional imaging for evaluation of GI NETs, PanNETs, and adrenal tumors. Multiphase (arterial and portal venous phase) is important because NETs frequently enhance in the arterial phase [NE-B 1 of 5]. Consider same imaging modality for follow-up studies.
staging.indication: All suspected or confirmed NETs; recommended baseline for all GI NETs, PanNETs, PCC/PGL, and ACC

staging.rationale: Evaluation for lung metastases and primary thoracic NETs. For lung/thymic NETs, chest CT with contrast is recommended. Also part of surveillance for all NETs.
staging.indication: All NETs at diagnosis and during surveillance; clinically indicated for GI NETs and PanNETs

staging.rationale: Functional imaging to assess somatostatin receptor status and distant disease. SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions is greater than liver. Essential for determining eligibility for PRRT with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate. Sensitivity and specificity for initial diagnosis of NETs: pooled 91% and 94% respectively [111]. Modified Krenning score (0–4) for qualitative assessment [NE-B 1 of 5]. Should be performed concurrently with diagnostic multiphase CT or MRI when possible.
staging.indication: Recommended for staging of GI NETs (especially jejunal/ileal/colon), PanNETs, PCC/PGL, and for PRRT eligibility assessment; also for surveillance with progressive disease on cross-sectional imaging

staging.rationale: Functional imaging for higher-grade or poorly differentiated tumors. May identify more aggressive disease. Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives more prognostic value than either alone [NE-B 1 of 5, references 1,2]. Consider in well-differentiated grade 3 NETs and when considering PRRT.
staging.indication: Well-differentiated grade 3 NETs, poorly differentiated NECs, suspected metastatic disease, bone-dominant PCC/PGL

staging.rationale: For gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs to determine depth of invasion and assess for lymphadenopathy. Also for pancreatic NETs to preoperatively assess and localize tumors.
staging.indication: Gastric NETs (all types for staging), duodenal NETs >1 cm or with concerning features, rectal NETs, PanNETs when resection is being considered

staging.rationale: Essential for evaluation of gastric NETs. Required to determine subtype (type 1, 2, 3, PPI-associated). High-definition white light endoscopy with video chromoendoscopy recommended. Biopsy protocol: minimum 2 biopsies from antrum (lesser and greater curvature), 1 from incisura, and 2 from gastric body [NET-1A footnote b, reference 10].
Evaluation for ileal and colon NETs, and to evaluate for synchronous lesions.
staging.indication: Suspected midgut/colonic NETs; as appropriate for duodenal NETs

staging.rationale: Staging for rectal NETs to assess depth of invasion and regional lymphadenopathy.
staging.indication: All rectal NETs (except small <1 cm completely resected incidental tumors with negative margins)

staging.test: Brain MRI or CT with contrast
staging.rationale: Evaluation for pituitary adenomas (MEN1) and brain metastases (poorly differentiated NECs). Generally not required for well-differentiated NETs [NE-B 1 of 5].
staging.indication: MEN1 patients for pituitary evaluation; poorly differentiated NECs of thorax and neck; as clinically indicated

staging.rationale: Evaluation for endobronchial lesions in suspected lung NETs.

For PCC/PGL: less sensitive than FDG-PET and 68Ga-DOTATATE for metastatic and multifocal PCC/PGL in patients with VHL and SDH syndromes [447]. MIBG scans should be obtained if considering treatment with I131-MIBG. SPECT/CT imaging of involved sites is recommended [PHEO-1 footnote j].
staging.indication: PCC/PGL when considering I131-MIBG therapy; if previous MIBG-positive scan or concern for disease progression

staging.test: Selective arterial calcium stimulation test (Imamura-Doppman procedure)
staging.rationale: For localization of insulinoma when imaging is negative or equivocal. Injection of calcium into selective pancreatic arteries with measurement of insulin levels in hepatic veins.
staging.indication: Persistent or recurrent insulinoma with negative or equivocal localization studies

staging.test: 4D-CT (four-dimensional computed tomography) or parathyroid sestamibi with SPECT/SPECT-CT
staging.rationale: Localization of parathyroid glands in hyperparathyroidism associated with MEN1. Sestamibi sensitivity ~35% in familial hyperparathyroidism with 4-gland hyperplasia [496,497]. 4D-CT has 60%–87% sensitivity [499]. For prolonged surveillance, radiation-free studies preferred.
staging.indication: MEN1 patients with elevated calcium and hyperparathyroidism

staging.rationale: Evaluation for carcinoid heart disease (CHD). Should include morphologic evaluation of valves (especially tricuspid and pulmonary), right heart size and function, and agitated saline injection if valve disease present to assess for atrial shunt [NE-B 2 of 5].
staging.indication: Baseline for all patients with carcinoid syndrome; every 1–3 years for patients without CHD; at least annually for patients with established CHD; prior to planned bowel/liver resection

biomarkers.rationale: Serotonin metabolite for monitoring carcinoid syndrome. Predictive role for mortality [135]. Decreasing levels indicate treatment response; increasing levels indicate treatment failure.
biomarkers.when: In patients with carcinoid syndrome at baseline and during follow-up. Not routinely recommended for asymptomatic patients, but may be considered for those with high burden of metastatic disease [NET-9].

biomarkers.rationale: Elevated in >60% of NET patients [238-240]. Associated with prognosis at levels ≥2× upper normal limit (HR, 2.8; 95% CI, 1.9–4.0; P < .001) [241]. Not recommended for routine follow-up for type 1 gastric NETs after baseline. Use limited by false elevations from PPIs, renal/hepatic impairment, and chronic gastritis.
biomarkers.when: At baseline in patients with nonfunctioning tumors; as clinically indicated during follow-up. Not recommended after baseline for type 1 gNETs.

biomarkers.test: Tumor-specific hormonal markers (gastrin, insulin, glucagon, VIP, somatostatin)
biomarkers.rationale: For functional PanNETs, follow-up with hormonal markers as clinically indicated for symptomatic monitoring and treatment response assessment.
biomarkers.when: At baseline and during surveillance for functional PanNETs; as clinically indicated.

biomarkers.rationale: Investigational biomarker. Presence of ≥1 CTC in 7.5 mL of blood independently associated with worse PFS and OS in patients with pretreated metastatic NETs [93].
biomarkers.when: Investigational; not currently part of routine care.

biomarkers.rationale: Blood-based test measuring multiple neuroendocrine gene transcripts. Demonstrated high sensitivity (>95%) in well-differentiated metastatic NETs [83]. Not impacted by PPI use unlike chromogranin A. Panel did not include in algorithm pending formal validation studies [MS-6].
biomarkers.when: Investigational; not currently recommended in guidelines.

do_not_miss: SSTR-based imaging (SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI) for all patients with well-differentiated NETs to assess receptor status and eligibility for PRRT with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate

Plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines in ALL patients with adrenal masses before any surgical procedure or invasive intervention (to rule out PCC/PGL)
Ki-67 proliferation index and/or mitotic rate on ALL NET specimens — must be reported in pathology; 'neuroendocrine tumor' without grade is inadequate for prognosis and therapy [NE-E 2 of 4]
Genetic counseling and testing for hereditary syndromes in all patients with ACC, PCC/PGL, MEN1/MEN2 features, multifocal PanNETs, gastrinomas, and thymic NETs
Echocardiography for all patients with carcinoid syndrome at baseline and periodically during follow-up
Molecular profiling (tissue NGS or ctDNA) for patients with unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy
Multiphasic (arterial + portal venous phase) contrast-enhanced CT or MRI for accurate assessment of hypervascular liver metastases

Adequate pathological assessment including tumor differentiation, WHO classification, Ki-67 index (preferred over mitotic rate unless insufficient tissue), mitotic rate, tumor size, margins, LVI, perineural invasion, lymph node status
Serum gastrin in patients with recurrent peptic ulcer disease, especially with diarrhea, before attributing to H. pylori alone
72-hour supervised fast with concurrent glucose, insulin, C-peptide, and pro-insulin for suspected insulinoma; rule out other causes of hypoglycemia first
CgA and hormonal markers as clinically indicated for functional tumors during follow-up
avoid: Routine SSTR-based imaging or FDG-PET/CT for surveillance after definitive resection of well-differentiated NETs [NET-8 footnote aa]
Chromogranin A alone as a screening test for NETs due to poor specificity and susceptibility to false elevations
Biopsy of suspected PCC/PGL without prior biochemical exclusion of PCC (risk of hypertensive crisis)
Omitting multiphasic CT/MRI protocol for NET liver metastasis evaluation (single-phase CT may miss hypervascular lesions that enhance in arterial phase)
Gastrin and chromogranin A level monitoring for type 1 gastric NETs after baseline (chronic hypergastrinemia from atrophic gastritis makes gastrin uninformative) [NET-8]
Using the term 'neuroendocrine tumor' or 'neuroendocrine carcinoma' without grade qualification in pathology reports — inadequate for prognosis and therapy [NE-E 2 of 4]
Staging PCC/PGL as 'benign' or 'malignant' in pathology — all PCCs and PGLs should be considered potentially malignant with lifelong risk of metastasis [NE-E 7 of 9]
Drug-eluting embolics for liver-directed therapy in NET patients (associated with increased hepatobiliary toxicity) [NE-H 1 of 2]

staging.system: AJCC 8th/9th edition TNM staging systems (Version 9, 2023 for GI and pancreatic NETs; Version 9, 2024 for lung and thymus; 8th edition, 2017 for adrenocortical carcinoma and PCC/PGL)

t_stages.description: Tumor invades mucosa or submucosa, and ≤1 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor invades muscularis propria or >1 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into subserosal tissue without penetration of overlying serosa
Tumor invades visceral peritoneum (serosal) or other organs or adjacent structures
Tumor invades mucosa or submucosa only and ≤1 cm (duodenal); ≤1 cm and confined within sphincter of Oddi (ampullary)
Tumor invades muscularis propria or >1 cm (duodenal); invades through sphincter into duodenal submucosa/muscularis propria or >1 cm (ampullary)
Tumor invades the pancreas or peripancreatic adipose tissue
Tumor invades the visceral peritoneum (serosa) or other organs
Tumor ≤2 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor >2 cm but ≤4 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor >4 cm in greatest dimension, or with subserosal invasion, or involvement of the mesoappendix
Tumor perforates the peritoneum, or directly invades other adjacent organs or structures (excluding direct mural extension to adjacent subserosa of adjacent bowel)
Tumor invades mucosa or submucosa and ≤2 cm; T1a ≤1 cm, T1b >1–≤2 cm
Tumor invades muscularis propria, or is >2 cm in greatest dimension with invasion of the mucosa or submucosa
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into subserosal tissue without penetration of overlying serosa
Tumor invades the visceral peritoneum (serosa), or other organs or adjacent structures
Tumor invades mucosa or submucosa and ≤1 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor invades muscularis propria or >1 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into subserosal tissue without penetration of overlying serosa
Tumor invades visceral peritoneum (serosa) or other organs or adjacent structures
Tumor limited to the pancreas, ≤2 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor limited to the pancreas, >2 cm but ≤4 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor limited to the pancreas, >4 cm in greatest dimension; or tumor invading the duodenum, ampulla of Vater, or common bile duct

Tumor invading adjacent organs (stomach, spleen, colon, adrenal gland) or the wall of large vessels (celiac axis or the superior mesenteric artery/vein, splenic artery/vein, gastroduodenal artery/vein, portal vein)
Tumor ≤3 cm; subcategories T1a (≤1 cm), T1b (>1–≤2 cm), T1c (>2–≤3 cm)
Tumor >3 cm but ≤5 cm; subcategories T2a (>3–≤4 cm), T2b (>4–≤5 cm)

Tumor >5 cm but ≤7 cm, or ≤7 cm with invasion of parietal pleura/chest wall, pericardium, phrenic nerve, azygos vein, thoracic nerve roots, stellate ganglion, or separate tumor nodule(s) in the same lobe

Tumor >7 cm, or tumor of any size invading mediastinum, thymus, trachea, carina, recurrent laryngeal nerve, vagus nerve, esophagus, diaphragm, heart, great vessels, subclavian vessels, vertebral body, spinal canal, cervical nerve roots, brachial plexus, or separate tumor nodule(s) in a different ipsilateral lobe
T1a: ≤5 cm, limited to thymus/encapsulation/mediastinal fat/mediastinal pleura only. T1b: >5 cm, same extent.
Direct invasion of pericardium (partial or full thickness), or lung, or phrenic nerve
Direct invasion into brachiocephalic vein, superior vena cava, chest wall, or extrapericardial pulmonary arteries or veins
Direct invasion into aorta (ascending, arch, descending), arch vessels, intrapericardial pulmonary artery or veins, myocardium, trachea, esophagus
Tumor ≤5 cm, no extra-adrenal invasion
Tumor >5 cm, no extra-adrenal invasion
Tumor of any size with local invasion but not invading adjacent organs/surrounding tissues
Tumor of any size that invades adjacent organs (kidney, diaphragm, pancreas, spleen, liver) or large blood vessels (renal vein or vena cava)
Pheochromocytoma <5 cm, no extra-adrenal invasion
Pheochromocytoma ≥5 cm or sympathetic paraganglioma of any size, no extra-adrenal invasion
Tumor of any size with local invasion into surrounding tissues (e.g., liver, pancreas, spleen, kidneys)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
No tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of fewer than 12 regional lymph nodes
Tumor involvement of large mesenteric masses (>2 cm) and/or extensive nodal deposits (≥12 regional lymph nodes), especially those encasing the superior mesenteric vessels
Tumor involvement of ipsilateral peribronchial and/or ipsilateral hilar and/or ipsilateral intrapulmonary lymph node station(s), including involvement by direct extension

Tumor involvement of ipsilateral mediastinal nodal station(s) and/or subcarinal lymph node station; N2a = single ipsilateral mediastinal station or subcarinal; N2b = multiple ipsilateral mediastinal stations
Tumor involvement of contralateral mediastinal, contralateral hilar, ipsilateral/contralateral scalene, or ipsilateral/contralateral supraclavicular lymph node station(s)
Tumor involvement of anterior (perithymic) lymph nodes
Tumor involvement of deep intrathoracic or cervical lymph nodes (e.g., paratracheal, subcarinal, aortopulmonary window, hilar, jugular, supraclavicular)

m_stages.description: Metastases in at least one extrahepatic site (e.g., lung, ovary, nonregional lymph node, peritoneum, bone)
Metastasis in pleural or pericardial nodules, and/or malignant pleural or pericardial effusions, and/or separate tumor nodule(s) in a contralateral lobe
Single extrathoracic metastasis in a single organ system (including a single non-regional node)
Multiple extrathoracic metastases in a single organ system
Multiple extrathoracic metastases in multiple organ systems
Pulmonary intraparenchymal nodule or other distant metastasis
Distant metastasis to only distant lymph nodes/liver or lung
Distant metastasis to bone plus multiple other sites

stage_groups.criteria: Jejunal/Ileal: T1 N0 M0; Duodenal/Ampulla: T1 NX/N0 M0; Appendix: T1 NX/N0 M0; Colon/Rectum: T1 NX/N0 M0; Stomach: T1 NX/N0 M0; Pancreas: T1 N0 M0; ACC: T1 N0 M0; PCC/PGL: T1 N0 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Localized tumor with limited invasion and no nodal or distant metastases. Generally favorable prognosis with surgical resection.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 92% (PanNETs, institutional data [71]); 100% (jejunal-ileocecal NETs [53]); 75%–95% for ACC depending on risk factors

stage_groups.criteria: Jejunal/Ileal: T2-T3 N0 M0; Duodenal/Ampulla: T2-T3 N0 M0; Appendix: T2-T3 NX/N0 M0; Colon/Rectum: T2 NX/N0 M0 (Stage IIA), T3 N0 M0 (Stage IIB); Stomach: T2-T3 N0 M0; Pancreas: T2-T3 N0 M0; ACC: T2 N0 M0; PCC/PGL: T2 N0 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Larger or more invasive tumor without nodal or distant metastases. Still potentially curative with resection.

stage_groups.criteria: Jejunal/Ileal: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1-N2 M0; Duodenal/Ampulla: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1 M0; Appendix: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1 M0; Colon/Rectum: T4 N0 M0 (Stage IIIA) or Any T N1 M0 (Stage IIIB); Stomach: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1 M0; Pancreas: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1 M0; Lung: T4 N0 M0 or T3-T4 N1 M0 (IIIA) etc.; ACC: T1-T2 N1 M0 or T3-T4 Any N M0; PCC/PGL: T1-T2 N1 M0 or T3 Any N M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease with nodal involvement or deep invasion. May be resectable with multimodal approach.

stage_groups.criteria: All sites: Any T, Any N, M1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastatic disease. Goals of treatment shift to palliation, symptom control, and extending survival.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 57% (PanNETs [71]); 72% (jejunal-ileocecal NETs [53]); 19.5% (PanNETs, SEER data [73]); <10% (ACC [21]); 63% at 5 years for PCC/PGL [442]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative: systemic therapy, liver-directed therapy, cytoreduction as appropriate

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastatic disease limited to contralateral lung/pleural/pericardial sites or single extrathoracic metastasis
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Varies by histology and extent of metastatic disease
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy, observation if low burden

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Multiple extrathoracic metastases in single or multiple organ systems
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Generally poor; dependent on histology and burden

staging_pearls: Multiple tumors in GI NETs should be designated as such; the largest tumor is used to assign T category (e.g., pT3(4) N0 M0 or pT3(m) N0 M0) [ST-1]
Mesenteric masses ≤2 cm in jejunal/ileal NETs should be stated in the pathology report but do not affect stage [ST-1 N footnote]

N2 category in jejunal/ileal NETs specifically includes large mesenteric masses >2 cm and/or extensive nodal deposits ≥12, especially those encasing superior mesenteric vessels — this is unique to small bowel NET staging [ST-1]

Extrapulmonary NECs (including MiNENs) arising in any site are staged according to organ-specific criteria for their non-neuroendocrine carcinoma counterparts (e.g., adenocarcinoma or squamous carcinoma), NOT by NET-specific staging [MS-5, reference 76]
For lung NETs, the staging system is the same as for more common lung carcinomas, incorporating both carcinomas and bronchopulmonary carcinoid (neuroendocrine) tumors [ST-6 to ST-10]
For thymic NETs, staging is based on thymus-specific TNM criteria, not the lung system [ST-11 to ST-12]
Parasympathetic paragangliomas are not staged because they are largely benign [ST-15 footnote]
PCC/PGL staging is for pheochromocytoma/sympathetic paraganglioma only; parasympathetic PGLs are not staged [ST-15]
ACC staging uses the 8th edition AJCC system and incorporates histologic grade (LG ≤20 mitoses/10 HPF; HG >20 mitoses/10 HPF or TP53/CTNNB mutations) [ST-16]
Ki-67 index and mitotic rate should both be reported; when discordant, the higher value determines grade classification [NE-E 2 of 4]
Ki-67 is preferred over mitotic rate for grading unless there is insufficient tissue [NE-E 2 of 4]
