Functional status is a clinical diagnosis, not a pathologic one — presence of hormone-staining granules without a clinical syndrome does not make a tumor 'functioning' [MS-6]
Well-differentiated G3 NETs (Ki-67 >20%, well-differentiated morphology) have intermediate prognosis compared to PDNECs and G1-G2 NETs; median OS of 41–99 months vs. 17 months for PDNECs [356,357]

Ki-67 threshold of 55% may better classify patients for response to platinum-based chemotherapy in high-grade GI NEC [51]; evolving data suggest Ki-67 20%–55% G3 NETs may not respond as well to platinum/etoposide as those with Ki-67 >55%

management_principles.overview: Neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) encompass a broad family of neoplasms arising from the diffuse endocrine system, including well-differentiated NETs (G1/G2/G3), poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas (PDNECs), and mixed neuroendocrine-non-neuroendocrine neoplasms (MiNENs). These guidelines address sporadic and hereditary neuroendocrine and adrenal tumors, with treatment approaches guided by tumor site of origin, histologic differentiation, grade/proliferation rate, stage, somatostatin receptor (SSTR) status, and hormonal functional status. Appropriate diagnosis and treatment often involves collaboration between specialists in multiple disciplines including pathologists, endocrinologists, radiologists (including nuclear medicine specialists), and medical, radiation, and surgical oncologists. The incidence of NENs has increased significantly over recent decades, with the 20-year forecasted prevalence estimated at 243,896 individuals in the United States as of January 2021 [5]. Well-differentiated NETs comprise a spectrum from low-grade (G1) to high-grade (G3), with prognosis varying by stage, grade, and primary site. Patients with NETs may present with symptoms of hormonal hypersecretion ('functional' tumors) or without such symptoms ('nonfunctional' tumors). All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized, resectable disease across all NET subtypes, including GI, lung, thymic, pancreatic, and adrenal primary tumors
by_intent.approach: Surgical resection is the mainstay of curative intent treatment. For most localized NETs, complete surgical resection with adequate regional lymph node dissection (including all palpable disease where feasible) is recommended. Specific procedures vary by primary site: anatomic resection with mediastinal lymph node dissection for lung NETs; partial or total gastrectomy with regional lymphadenectomy for type 3 gastric NETs; bowel resection with lymphadenectomy for small bowel NETs; endoscopic resection for selected duodenal and rectal NETs; and site-specific pancreatic surgery for PanNETs. For adrenocortical carcinoma, achieving R0 resection is of critical importance and these operations should be performed by surgeons with high-volume experience in adrenal surgery [NE-D 2 of 3]. There is no established role for adjuvant therapy outside of clinical trials for NETs, although adjuvant mitotane therapy can be considered for high-risk adrenocortical carcinoma [NE-G 1 of 11, AGT-5].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with locoregional unresectable or metastatic disease, including both functional and nonfunctional tumors
by_intent.approach: Treatment focuses on controlling tumor growth, managing hormonal symptoms, and maintaining quality of life. Somatostatin analogs (octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide) serve dual purposes: symptom control for functional tumors and antiproliferative effects for SSTR-positive tumors. The PROMID trial demonstrated an antiproliferative effect of octreotide acetate LAR in advanced midgut NETs [158], and the CLARINET trial showed similar benefits with lanreotide in advanced well-differentiated GEP-NETs [160]. For tumors with clinically significant progression, molecularly targeted therapies (everolimus, sunitinib, cabozantinib), peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT), cytotoxic chemotherapy, and locoregional therapies (liver-directed therapy, radiation therapy) are employed. The choice of therapy considers tumor biology, SSTR status, Ki-67 index, tumor burden, patient performance status, and prior treatment history.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with resected adrenocortical carcinoma at high risk for recurrence
by_intent.approach: There is no established role for adjuvant systemic therapy in NETs outside of clinical trials. For adrenocortical carcinoma, adjuvant mitotane therapy can be considered for patients at high risk for local recurrence based on features including positive margins, Ki-67 >10%, rupture of capsule, large size, and high grade. Mitotane blood levels should be monitored with target levels of 14–20 mcg/mL if tolerated. Life-long hydrocortisone ± fludrocortisone replacement is usually required. The role of adjuvant chemotherapy is under investigation in the ongoing ADIUVO-2 study (NCT03583710) [NE-G 8 of 11, AGT-5]. Adjuvant external beam radiation therapy to the tumor bed can also be considered if concern exists regarding tumor spillage or close margins after surgery [AGT-5].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary discussion is recommended for all patients with neuroendocrine and adrenal tumors. A multidisciplinary approach is essential for treatment planning, particularly for complex presentations including unresectable locoregional disease, metastatic disease, functional tumors requiring hormonal management, and hereditary syndromes requiring genetic counseling. Specific multidisciplinary discussions are recommended prior to biopsy of pancreatic NETs [PanNET-3 footnote l], for management of large (>2 cm) type 3 gastric NETs [NET-1], for ampullary duodenal NETs [NE-C 3 of 6], and for patients with well-differentiated Grade 3 NETs with unfavorable biology [WDG3-2]. For patients with carcinoid syndrome, invasive procedures should only be performed in centers with experienced anesthesiologists [NE-K 1 of 3]. The Panel acknowledges that unusual patient scenarios (presenting in <5% of patients) are not specifically discussed in these guidelines.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is a critical consideration in treatment selection for neuroendocrine and adrenal tumors. Patients with good performance status are candidates for aggressive multimodal approaches including surgical resection, cytoreductive surgery, and combination systemic therapies. For patients with resectable locoregional disease, surgery is recommended if the patient can tolerate the procedure. For metastatic disease, treatment considerations include performance status alongside tumor biology, burden of disease, and rate of growth. Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve progression-free survival, particularly for patients with relatively indolent metastatic small bowel NETs [NE-D 2 of 3]. For adrenocortical carcinoma, patients who undergo splenectomy should receive vaccination against encapsulated bacteria [NE-D 2 of 3]. Patients with PCCs/PGLs require appropriate alpha-adrenergic blockade with volume repletion for 7–14 days prior to surgical therapy [PHEO-2]. For patients with other life-limiting comorbidities or high surgical risk, observation may be appropriate for small, indolent tumors [PanNET-2].

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Neuroendocrine Tumors of the GI Tract, Lung, and Thymus (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2)
branching_basis: primary site (gastric, duodenal, appendiceal, jejunal/ileal/colon, rectal, lung, thymus)

options.label: Endoscopic treatment
options.recommendation: For tumors ≤1 cm: surveillance or endoscopic resection (polypectomy or EMR for mucosal lesions). For tumors >1 but ≤2 cm: consider EUS, surveillance or endoscopic resection (EMR for mucosal; ESD or eFTR for submucosal). For tumors >2 cm: EUS, cross-sectional imaging, multidisciplinary discussion including surgical evaluation [NE-C 1 of 6, NET-1]

options.label: Endoscopic treatment and gastrinoma management
options.recommendation: Endoscopic resection of gastric tumors >1 cm and multidisciplinary approach to treat the underlying gastrinoma (see PanNET-3 for identification and treatment of gastrin-producing tumor) [NET-1]

options.label: Partial or total gastrectomy with regional lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Partial or total gastrectomy (based on tumor location) with regional lymphadenectomy (preferred), or if no evidence of regional lymphadenopathy on EUS or other imaging, consider endoscopic resection or surgical wedge resection [NET-1]

options.label: Endoscopic resection
options.recommendation: For non-invasive tumors: endoscopic resection. For invasive tumors: local excision (transduodenal) with regional lymphadenectomy or pancreatoduodenectomy [NET-2]

nodes.criteria: Appendiceal NETs - Tumor ≤2 cm, simple appendectomy performed

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: For tumor <1 cm: no surveillance indicated. For tumors ≥1 to ≤2 cm: optional surveillance every 2–5 years based on clinicopathologic features [NET-3]

nodes.criteria: Appendiceal NETs - Tumor >2 cm or any size with incomplete resection or positive lymph nodes

options.label: Right hemicolectomy
options.recommendation: Consider right hemicolectomy. Greater than 12 lymph nodes should be retrieved. For any tumor size with incomplete resection or positive lymph nodes within the appendectomy specimen, recommend complete staging imaging and proceed to right hemicolectomy [NET-3]

options.label: Bowel resection with regional lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Bowel resection(s) with regional lymphadenectomy. Include manual palpation of entire bowel for synchronous tumors, assess proximity to superior mesenteric vessels, resection to root of mesentery [NET-4]

nodes.criteria: Rectal NETs - Small (<1 cm), completely resected, incidental, negative margins, low grade

options.label: Endoscopic surveillance
options.recommendation: Endoscopic surveillance [NET-5]

options.label: Transanal or endoscopic excision
options.recommendation: Resection (transanal or endoscopic excision, if possible). For 1- to 2-cm tumors, consider examination under anesthesia and/or EUS with radical resection if muscularis propria invasion or node positive [NET-5]

nodes.criteria: Rectal NETs - T2–T4 or node positive, >2 cm

options.label: Low anterior resection or abdominoperineal resection
options.recommendation: Low anterior resection or abdominoperineal resection. In selected cases, there may be a role for neoadjuvant or definitive chemoradiation [NET-5]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Neoadjuvant or definitive chemoradiation may be considered in selected cases [NET-5]

options.label: Lobectomy or other anatomic resection
options.recommendation: Lobectomy or other anatomic resection + mediastinal node dissection or sampling. If surgery contraindicated, thermal ablation or stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) [NET-6]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy recommended for localized disease

options.label: Surgical resection
options.recommendation: Resect (surgical resection) [NET-7]

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Advanced Disease and/or Distant Metastases of the GI Tract (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2 Neuroendocrine Tumors)

options.label: Surgical treatment of primary and distant disease
options.recommendation: Surgical treatment of primary and distant disease. Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve progression-free survival [NET-9, NE-D 2 of 3]

nodes.criteria: Surgical cytoreduction of metastases not possible - Low tumor burden

options.label: Observation or Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide
options.recommendation: Consider resection of primary tumor (particularly if symptomatic) and/or observe with imaging or octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide. For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks [NET-9]

nodes.criteria: Clinically significant tumor burden - First-line treatment

options.label: Systemic therapy options
options.recommendation: Preferred: Cabozantinib (category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus or Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate) [1]; Everolimus (category 1 for nonfunctional tumors) [2,3]; PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive and progression on Octreotide/Lanreotide, category 1 for progressive mid-gut tumors) [NE-J]; First-line PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive, Ki-67 ≥10%, clinically significant tumor burden) [4]; Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms) [5-8], [NE-G 1 of 11]

options.label: Subsequent systemic therapy options
options.recommendation: Consider clinical trial, locoregional therapy options (liver-directed therapy for liver-predominant disease, RT for locally advanced unresectable disease, palliative RT for oligometastatic disease), or change to alternate systemic therapy [NET-10]

nodes.criteria: Asymptomatic, low tumor burden, low grade (typical carcinoid)

options.label: Observation or Octreotide acetate LAR/Lanreotide
options.recommendation: Observe or Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms). Monitor with Chest CT with contrast and multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI every 3–6 months [NET-12]

nodes.criteria: Clinically significant tumor burden, low grade, or evidence of disease progression, or intermediate grade (atypical carcinoid), or symptomatic disease

options.label: Clinical trial, Observation (select patients), or Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial (preferred) or Observation in select patients or Systemic therapy (Cabozantinib category 1 if prior Everolimus; Everolimus category 1 for nonfunctional; Octreotide/Lanreotide if SSTR+) or locoregional therapy (liver-directed therapy for liver-predominant disease) [NET-12, NE-G 2 of 11]

management_pathways.title: Non-Metastatic Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2)
branching_basis: clinical scenario (nonfunctioning, gastrinoma, insulinoma, glucagonoma, VIPoma)

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation can be considered for small (≤2 cm), low-grade, incidentally discovered, non-functional tumors. Decision based on estimated surgical risk, site of tumor, and patient comorbidities [PanNET-2]

options.label: Observation or Pancreatectomy
options.recommendation: Observation or Pancreatectomy (NE-D). Grade 1 PanNETs <2 cm can be safely observed and is the recommended treatment course. Grade 2 PanNETs should be considered for surgical resection if patient factors allow [PanNET-2, NE-D 1 of 3]

nodes.criteria: Nonfunctioning PanNET >2 cm, invasive, or node-positive

options.label: Pancreatectomy with regional lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Pancreatectomy (NE-D). Tumors of the head: pancreatoduodenectomy. Tumors of body/tail: distal pancreatectomy with or without splenectomy or spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy [PanNET-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy recommended. There is no established role for adjuvant therapy [PanNET-2]

nodes.criteria: Resectable tumors in setting of germline VHL alteration

options.label: Consider belzutifan
options.recommendation: Consider belzutifan for resectable tumors in the setting of germline VHL alteration. The decision to use belzutifan in small resectable tumors needs to be individualized [PanNET-2]

nodes.criteria: Gastrinoma - Occult, no primary tumor or metastases on imaging

options.label: Exploratory surgery with duodenotomy
options.recommendation: Observation or exploratory surgery including duodenotomy and intraoperative ultrasound; local resection/enucleation of tumor(s) + periduodenal node dissection. Manage gastric hypersecretion with high-dose PPIs and Octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide [PanNET-4]

options.label: Resection
options.recommendation: Primary located → Resection (NE-D) [PanNET-4]

options.label: Tumor enucleation
options.recommendation: Tumor enucleation (NE-D). Stabilize glucose levels with diet and/or diazoxide and/or everolimus. For deeper or invasive tumors, pancreatoduodenectomy (head) or distal pancreatectomy (distal) [PanNET-6]

options.label: Pancreatoduodenectomy or distal pancreatectomy
options.recommendation: Head: Pancreatoduodenectomy + peripancreatic lymphadenectomy. Distal: Distal pancreatectomy + peripancreatic lymphadenectomy + splenectomy. Treat hyperglycemia and diabetes. Consider perioperative anticoagulation due to hypercoagulable state [PanNET-8]

options.label: Pancreatoduodenectomy or distal pancreatectomy
options.recommendation: Head: Pancreatoduodenectomy + peripancreatic lymphadenectomy. Distal: Distal pancreatectomy + peripancreatic lymphadenectomy ± splenectomy. Octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide. Correct electrolyte imbalance (K+, Mg2+, HCO3-) and dehydration [PanNET-10]

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Advanced and/or Metastatic Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2)

options.label: Resection of metastases and primary
options.recommendation: Resect metastases and primary if possible and clinically indicated (NE-D). Staged or synchronous resection when possible. When performing staged pancreatoduodenectomy and liver resection, consider hepatectomy prior to pancreatic resection [PanNET-12]

nodes.criteria: Asymptomatic, low tumor burden, stable disease

options.label: Observation with Octreotide/Lanreotide
options.recommendation: Observe with markers and multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI every 12 wk–12 mo. Consider octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide (NE-G) [PanNET-12]

nodes.criteria: Symptomatic, clinically significant tumor burden, or clinically significant progressive disease - First-line

options.label: Systemic therapy options
options.recommendation: Preferred: Cabozantinib (category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus, Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or Sunitinib); Everolimus 10 mg PO daily (category 1 for progressive disease); Sunitinib 37.5 mg PO daily (category 1 for progressive disease); Capecitabine/Temozolomide (preferred when tumor response needed); Octreotide/Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive); First-line PRRT (if SSTR-positive, Ki-67 ≥10%); PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive and progression on Octreotide/Lanreotide) [NE-G 3 of 11]

nodes.criteria: Clinically significant progressive disease - Subsequent therapy

options.label: Subsequent systemic therapy or locoregional therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial or Systemic therapy (NE-G) or locoregional therapy options (liver-directed therapy for liver-predominant disease, RT ± concurrent fluoropyrimidine for locally advanced unresectable disease, palliative RT for oligometastatic disease) [PanNET-13]

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Disease (Resectable) - Well-Differentiated Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors

nodes.criteria: Favorable biology (Ki-67 <55%, slow growing, positive SSTR-PET)

options.label: Resection + regional lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Resection + regional lymphadenectomy if feasible [WDG3-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: There is no role for adjuvant therapy outside of a clinical trial [WDG3-2]

nodes.criteria: Unfavorable biology (Ki-67 ≥55%, faster growing, negative SSTR-PET)

options.label: Clinical trial, resection after MDT review, or neoadjuvant therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial (preferred) or Resection with regional lymphadenectomy if appropriate after multidisciplinary review, or Neoadjuvant systemic therapy on a case-by-case basis (eg, Ki-67 ≥55%) [WDG3-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: There is no role for adjuvant therapy outside of a clinical trial [WDG3-2]

management_pathways.title: Locally Advanced/Metastatic Disease - Well-Differentiated Grade 3 NETs with Favorable Biology

options.label: Resection of primary + metastatic sites
options.recommendation: Resection of primary + metastatic sites, if feasible, with acceptable risk and toxicity profile [WDG3-3]

options.label: Observation, Octreotide/Lanreotide, PRRT, or palliative RT
options.recommendation: Observation with short-interval follow-up, or Octreotide/Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms), or PRRT with lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive, category 2B), or Palliative RT for oligometastatic disease [WDG3-3]

nodes.criteria: Unresectable - Clinically significant tumor burden or disease progression

options.label: Systemic therapy or locoregional therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial (preferred) or Systemic therapy options (Cabozantinib, Capecitabine/Temozolomide, PRRT if SSTR+, Temozolomide, CAPEOX, Everolimus, FOLFOX, Octreotide/Lanreotide) or locoregional therapy options [WDG3-3, NE-G 4 of 11]

management_pathways.title: Locally Advanced/Metastatic Disease - Well-Differentiated Grade 3 NETs with Unfavorable Biology

nodes.criteria: Unfavorable biology (Ki-67 ≥55%, rapid growth, FDG-avid, SSTR-negative)

options.label: Systemic therapy or locoregional therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial (preferred) or Systemic therapy options (Carboplatin/Etoposide, Cisplatin/Etoposide, Oxaliplatin-based therapy [CAPEOX, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX], Capecitabine/Temozolomide, Irinotecan-based therapy, Temozolomide, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab [category 2B]) or locoregional therapy options, or Resection may play a role in select cases [WDG3-4, NE-G 5 of 11]

management_pathways.title: Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs

nodes.criteria: SSTR-negative, non-functional, surgical cytoreduction recommended with low tumor burden

options.label: Observe with imaging
options.recommendation: Observe with imaging. Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3–6 months) recommended because SSTR-negative disease has a worse prognosis [SSTRNEG-1, Refardt et al. 2020]

nodes.criteria: Non-functional with clinically significant tumor burden or disease progression

options.label: Systemic therapy excluding PRRT
options.preference: Systemic therapy based on tumor type
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy based on tumor type (excluding PRRT) (NE-G). PRRT can be considered for mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease but might not work as well [SSTRNEG-1]

options.label: Octreotide/Lanreotide followed by subsequent therapy
options.recommendation: First line: Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide. Subsequent-line: Systemic therapy based on tumor type (excluding PRRT) [SSTRNEG-1]

management_pathways.title: Extrapulmonary Poorly Differentiated Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (MiNEN)
branching_basis: molecular alterations (MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H, NTRK, BRAF, RET)

options.label: Resection + adjuvant chemotherapy ± RT
options.recommendation: Options may include: Resection + adjuvant chemotherapy ± RT; Neoadjuvant chemotherapy ± RT + resection; Chemotherapy alone; or Definitive chemoradiation with cisplatin + etoposide or carboplatin + etoposide [PDNEC-1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy (typically cisplatin/etoposide or carboplatin/etoposide based) is recommended after resection, similar to small cell lung cancer paradigm [PDNEC-1]

options.label: Concurrent or sequential RT + chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Concurrent or sequential RT + chemotherapy. Options: Carboplatin/Etoposide, Cisplatin/Etoposide, or Capecitabine (when etoposide/platinum not feasible) [PDNEC-1, NE-G 6 of 11]

options.label: Systemic chemotherapy ± immunotherapy
options.recommendation: Preferred: Carboplatin/Etoposide [32] or Cisplatin/Etoposide [16]. Other Recommended: Capecitabine/Temozolomide, FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, Temozolomide. For metastatic disease with progression: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab [category 2B]. Immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab if MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H. Targeted therapy based on molecular alterations (NTRK fusions, BRAF V600E, RET fusions) [PDNEC-1, NE-G 6 of 11]

nodes.criteria: Resectable disease - all hormonally secreting PCCs/PGLs

options.label: Resection (minimally invasive preferred)
options.recommendation: Resection (minimally invasive preferred when safe and feasible). Requires preoperative alpha blockade with volume repletion and high salt diet for 7–14 days or until stable. Consider adding dihydropyridine calcium channel blockade, beta blockade, or metyrosine as needed [PHEO-2]

options.label: Observe
options.recommendation: Observe, if asymptomatic or slow-growing, low-volume disease [PHEO-2]

options.label: Continue alpha blockade and clinical trial or systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Continue alpha blockade and: Clinical trial or Cytoreductive (R2) resection when possible or Systemic therapy or Palliative RT for oligometastatic disease. Systemic therapy options: Belzutifan, Cabozantinib, Sunitinib 37.5 mg daily, Systemic chemotherapy (CVD or Temozolomide), PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive), SSAs (if SSTR-positive), Selpercatinib (if RET gene fusion-positive) [PHEO-2, NE-G 7 of 11]

options.label: Open adrenalectomy
options.recommendation: Resect tumor and radiographically or clinically evident lymph nodes (open adrenalectomy recommended). May require removal of adjacent structures. Achieving R0 resection is of critical importance [AGT-5, NE-D 2 of 3]

adjuvant_logic.summary: For high risk of local recurrence (positive margins, Ki-67 >10%, rupture of capsule, large size, high grade): Clinical trial (preferred), consider EBRT to tumor bed, or consider adjuvant Mitotane therapy [AGT-5]

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preferred: Carboplatin/Etoposide ± Doxorubicin ± Mitotane or Cisplatin/Etoposide ± Doxorubicin ± Mitotane. Other Recommended: Cabozantinib, Mitotane monotherapy, Mitotane + Pembrolizumab, Pembrolizumab. Useful in certain circumstances: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab, Osilodrostat (for symptom control). Consider resection if >90% removable after response to systemic therapy [AGT-5, NE-G 8 of 11]

management_pathways.title: Neuroendocrine Neoplasms of Unknown Primary - Well-Differentiated
branching_basis: primary site identified vs not identified

options.label: Treatment as locoregional advanced/metastatic GI tract NET
options.recommendation: Management of locoregional advanced disease and/or distant metastases of the gastrointestinal tract [NET-9], [NUP-1]

options.label: Treatment as G3 NET (favorable or unfavorable biology)
options.recommendation: Management of well-differentiated grade 3 locoregional disease (WDG3-2) and locally advanced/metastatic disease (WDG3-3 or WDG3-4) [NUP-1]

options.label: Treatment as extrapulmonary PDNEC
options.recommendation: See Extrapulmonary Poorly Differentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm [PDNEC-1], [NUP-1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy may be indicated for resected PDNEC [PDNEC-1]

options.label: Subtotal parathyroidectomy or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation
options.recommendation: Subtotal parathyroidectomy ± cryopreservation of parathyroids ± thymectomy, OR Total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation ± cryopreservation of parathyroids ± thymectomy. Studies showed better prognosis when thymectomy is done at same time as partial parathyroidectomy [8], [MEN1-2, NE-F 6 of 6]

options.label: Bilateral adrenalectomy
options.recommendation: For synchronous bilateral PCCs, a bilateral adrenalectomy is recommended. As patients with MEN2 have an appreciable risk for bilateral tumors, a cortical-sparing adrenalectomy may be considered [MEN2-1, NE-F 5 of 6]

options.label: Adrenalectomy (cortical-sparing consideration)
options.recommendation: Resection of PCC should take priority over thyroidectomy for MTC when synchronous. Cortical-sparing adrenalectomy may be considered given appreciable risk for bilateral tumors [MEN2-1]

items.text: Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of primary site with arterial and portal venous phases
items.recommendation: Recommended for all NET patients. Essential for liver metastasis evaluation as NETs frequently enhance in arterial phase [NE-B 1 of 5]

items.text: Chest CT ± contrast per institutional standards
items.recommendation: Recommended without known tumor or specific clinical concern; brain imaging generally not required for well-differentiated NET [NE-B 1 of 5]

items.text: Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI
items.recommendation: Recommended for GI NETs, PanNETs. Should include primary site of disease [NE-B 1 of 5]

items.text: Consider same imaging modality and contrast agent for follow-up studies
items.recommendation: For consistent imaging interpretation [NE-B 1 of 5]

Recommended for assessment of SSTR status, particularly for midgut NETs and when PRRT is being considered. Should be performed with multiphase IV contrast when possible. SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions greater than liver [NE-B 1 of 5]
For qualitative lesion assessment: 0 = no uptake, 1 = very low uptake, 2 = uptake ≤ liver, 3 = uptake > liver, 4 = uptake > spleen [NE-B 1 of 5]
Considered in select cases where G2 or higher NETs or NECs is documented. Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives more prognostic value and may identify more active disease [NE-B 1 of 5]
Not part of routine evaluation of PDNECs [PDNEC-1]

Recommended for carcinoid syndrome evaluation in patients with symptoms of flushing and diarrhea, particularly with ileal, jejunal, and cecal NETs. Foods to avoid for 48 hours prior: avocados, bananas, cantaloupe, eggplant, pineapples, plums, tomatoes, hickory nuts/pecans, plantains, kiwi, dates, grapefruit, honeydew, walnuts [NE-A 1 of 4]

Recommended for evaluation of gastric NETs and gastrinomas. Should be checked when fasting and off PPI for >1 week. PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms of gastrinoma and/or risks of complications [NET-1 footnote c]

items.text: Serum insulin, pro-insulin, and C-peptide during concurrent hypoglycemia
items.recommendation: Recommended for evaluation of insulinoma. Insulin level >3 mcIU/mL, C-peptide ≥0.6 ng/mL, proinsulin ≥5 pmol/L when fasting glucose <55 mg/dL is suspicious [PanNET-5, NE-A 1 of 4]
Elevated in ≥60% of patients with NETs; associated with shorter survival. However, elevated in renal/hepatic impairment and PPI use; should not be relied upon in isolation [NE-A 1 of 4]

items.text: Plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated total metanephrines and catecholamines
items.recommendation: Recommended for PCC/PGL evaluation. Elevations 3 times above upper limit of normal are diagnostic. Concurrent medications should be reviewed [PHEO-1, NE-A 2 of 4]

items.text: Biochemical evaluation for primary aldosteronism, hypercortisolemia, and androgen excess
items.recommendation: Recommended for all adrenal masses. For hypercortisolemia: 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test, 2–3 midnight salivary cortisols, or 24-hour urinary free cortisol [AGT-1, NE-A 2 of 4]

items.text: Ki-67 proliferative index and/or mitotic rate
items.recommendation: Required for all NET specimens. Ki-67 should be performed on primary and metastatic specimens. Report actual parameters used to assign grade [NE-E 1 of 9]

items.text: Broad-spectrum keratin marker (to distinguish PCC/PGL), synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and INSM1
items.recommendation: Required immunohistochemistry markers for neuroendocrine differentiation [NE-E 1 of 9]
Recommended for well-differentiated Grade 3 NETs to distinguish from large cell NEC. Assessment of p53, Rb, p16 can be considered if uncertain about differentiation [WDG3-1]

Recommended for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy. Consider testing for NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E mutations, MSI-H, dMMR, and TMB-H [PDNEC-1, NE-G 6 of 11]

items.text: Genetic counseling and testing for inherited genetic syndromes
items.recommendation: Recommended for all patients with ACC, PCC/PGL, gastrinoma, multifocal PanNETs, clinical suspicion for MEN1 or MEN2. Should be considered for duodenal NET/PanNET at any age, thymic NET at any age [NE-F 1 of 6]
Can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible for identifying actionable alterations [PDNEC-1]

Recommended for patients with suspected carcinoid syndrome to assess for carcinoid heart disease (CHD). Should include morphologic evaluation of valves (tricuspid and pulmonary) and right heart size/function [NET-14, NE-B 2 of 5]

surgery.role: Surgical resection is the primary curative treatment for localized NETs and plays an important role in cytoreduction for selected patients with metastatic disease. For most localized NETs, complete surgical resection with adequate regional lymph node dissection is recommended. Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve progression-free survival, particularly for patients with relatively indolent metastatic small bowel NETs [NE-D 2 of 3].
principles: Resection of GI NETs should include adequate regional lymph node resection (including all palpable disease where feasible). Palpate the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve [NE-D 1 of 3]
Gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs may be endoscopically resectable [NE-C]
Standard oncologic surgery (distal pancreatectomy/splenectomy or pancreatoduodenectomy) is appropriate for most resectable, non-metastatic PanNETs [NE-D 1 of 3]

For non-functional PanNETs <2 cm, individualized decisions based on tumor characteristics and patient factors are recommended. Grade 1 PanNETs <2 cm can be safely observed; Grade 2 PanNETs should be considered for surgical resection [NE-D 1 of 3]
After simple appendectomy, if tumor >2 cm and positive lymph nodes in specimen, complete staging imaging and right hemicolectomy recommended [NE-D 1 of 3]
Assess implications of pancreatoduodenectomy in metastatic disease - usually not curative and impacts liver-directed therapy long-term [NE-D 1 of 3]
In setting of metastatic disease, resection of ileum/jejunum NETs should be performed when symptoms arise or to reduce future obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, bleeding, or perforation [NE-D 2 of 3]
Cholecystectomy is recommended when performing surgery for advanced NETs in patients anticipated to receive long-term SSAs due to higher risk of biliary symptoms [NE-D 2 of 3]

Prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide administration should not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration [NE-D 2 of 3, NE-K 1 of 3]

For patients with clinical stage I or II ACC, achieving R0 resection is of critical importance. Operations should be performed by surgeons with high-volume experience. Open approach typically advised [NE-D 2 of 3]

procedures.name: Lobectomy or other anatomic resection with mediastinal node dissection/sampling
procedures.indications: Localized and resectable lung NETs. Sublobar resection may yield similar outcomes even for atypical carcinoids if tumor can be resected completely [NE-D 1 of 3, NET-6]
procedures.key_complications: Surgical site infection, bleeding, air leak, pneumonia

procedures.name: Partial or total gastrectomy with regional lymphadenectomy
procedures.indications: Type 3 (sporadic) gastric NETs, type 1 or 2 gastric NETs >2 cm [NET-1]
procedures.key_complications: Dumping syndrome, anastomotic leak, nutritional deficiencies

procedures.name: Pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure)
procedures.indications: PanNETs of the pancreatic head, duodenal/periampullary NETs not amenable to endoscopic/local excision, gastrinomas of the pancreatic head [NE-D 1 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic leak, delayed gastric emptying, bleeding, infection

procedures.name: Distal pancreatectomy with or without splenectomy
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive; spleen-preserving when possible
procedures.indications: PanNETs of the pancreatic body or tail [NE-D 1 of 3]

procedures.name: Tumor enucleation
procedures.indications: Exophytic or peripheral insulinomas, selected small PanNETs [NE-D 1 of 3, PanNET-6]

procedures.name: Bowel resection with regional lymphadenectomy
procedures.approach: Open or hybrid minimally invasive with manual palpation

procedures.name: Right hemicolectomy
procedures.indications: Appendiceal NETs >2 cm, or any size with incomplete resection or positive lymph nodes [NET-3]

procedures.name: Low anterior resection or abdominoperineal resection
procedures.indications: Rectal NETs T2–T4 or node positive [NET-5]
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, sexual dysfunction, bladder dysfunction

procedures.name: Minimally invasive adrenalectomy
procedures.indications: Benign adrenal tumors, PCCs (preferred when safe and feasible), small ACC (explore with minimally invasive with planned conversion if invasion suspected) [AGT-3, AGT-4, PHEO-2]
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, adrenal insufficiency, tumor capsule breach risk

procedures.name: Open adrenalectomy
procedures.indications: Suspected ACC, large adrenal tumors (>4 cm with malignant features), locally invasive disease. May require removal of adjacent structures [AGT-4, AGT-5, NE-D 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Tumor spillage risk if capsule breached, adjacent organ injury

procedures.name: Subtotal or cortical-sparing adrenalectomy
procedures.indications: MEN2-associated PCCs with bilateral tumors, hereditary PCC/PGL syndromes with appreciable risk for bilateral tumors [NE-F 5 of 6, MEN2-1]

procedures.name: Cytoreductive hepatectomy
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive with parenchymal sparing preferred
procedures.indications: Liver metastases from NETs where >90% cytoreduction is feasible, particularly for symptomatic patients or those with relatively indolent disease [NE-D 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Liver failure, bile leak, bleeding, infection

procedures.name: Parathyroidectomy (subtotal or total with autotransplantation)
procedures.key_complications: Hypoparathyroidism (1%–5%), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (~1%), bleeding

surgery.resectability_criteria: For NETs, resectability is determined by the ability to achieve complete (R0) resection with acceptable morbidity. Locoregional resectable disease includes tumors confined to the primary organ with or without regional lymph node involvement. Key factors include: tumor size, depth of invasion, lymph node status, distant metastases, involvement of major vessels, and patient performance status. For small bowel NETs, resectability includes assessment of proximity to superior mesenteric artery and vein. For PanNETs, resectability considers involvement of adjacent organs or large vessels. For ACC, achieving R0 resection is of critical importance and operations should be performed by surgeons with high-volume experience in adrenal surgery [NE-D 2 of 3]. In the setting of metastatic disease, cytoreductive resection of >90% is considered optimal; if <90% removable, surgery can be reconsidered following response to systemic therapy [AGT-5]. Patients with PCCs/PGLs require preoperative alpha blockade for 7–14 days prior to surgery [PHEO-2].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Preoperative optimization varies by tumor type: For carcinoid syndrome: prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis; vasopressor use and fluid administration should not be delayed [NE-D 2 of 3, NE-K 1 of 3]. For PCCs/PGLs: Alpha-adrenergic blockade with aggressive volume repletion and high-salt diet for 7–14 days or until stable. Consider adding dihydropyridine calcium channel blockade, beta blockade, or metyrosine as needed [PHEO-2]. For insulinomas: Stabilize glucose levels with diet (complex carbohydrates) and/or diazoxide and/or everolimus [PanNET-6]. For glucagonomas: Treat hyperglycemia and diabetes. Consider perioperative anticoagulation due to hypercoagulable state (10%–33%) [PanNET-8]. For VIPomas: Correct electrolyte imbalance (K+, Mg2+, HCO3-) and dehydration [PanNET-10]. All patients who might require splenectomy should receive preoperative vaccination against pneumococcus, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and meningococcal group C at least 14 days before elective splenectomy [NE-D 2 of 3]. For ACC: Perioperative stress-dose steroids should be included [AGT-3 footnote l].

radiation_therapy.role: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) is a useful modality for select patients with locoregional or metastatic neuroendocrine or adrenal tumors. Decisions to use EBRT should be made in a multidisciplinary manner, considering patient factors, disease site/stage, and other therapeutic options available [NE-I 1 of 2].
principles: EBRT may be considered for any histologic subtype of NETs (including well-differentiated G1/2/3, NECs, MiNENs, PCCs, and PGLs) [NE-I 1 of 2]
Site-specific principles used for other primary cancer types are generally applicable [NE-I 1 of 2]
Higher EBRT doses have been associated with higher rates of disease control, but may be associated with increased risk of adverse events [3], [NE-I 1 of 2]
IMRT/VMAT techniques with IGRT may be needed to maintain precision and reduce toxicity [NE-I 1 of 2]
When administering SBRT/SABR, IGRT techniques and motion management are strongly recommended [NE-I 1 of 2]

EBRT generally does not play a role in locoregional NETs of small bowel, appendix, or colon (including mesenteric disease) due to risk of bowel injury. However, focal EBRT may be considered in select circumstances [NE-I 1 of 2]
EBRT may be considered in locoregional NETs of lung, thymus, and GI tract locations where resection may have significant morbidity [NE-I 1 of 2]

dose_frameworks.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)/Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Varies by site and organ tolerance
dose_frameworks.indication: Oligometastatic disease at multiple sites including liver, adrenal, bone, lung, mediastinum, head and neck, and lymph nodes; liver-limited metastases with <5 lesions [NE-I 1 of 2]

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionated RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Most frequently used regimen for functional NETs to improve hormonal symptoms [NE-I 1 of 2]

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional fractionation with concurrent chemotherapy
dose_frameworks.indication: Locoregional unresectable disease, particularly for atypical carcinoid histology or tumors with higher Ki-67 [NET-11]

approaches.name: Palliative RT for symptomatic bone metastases
approaches.indication: Painful bone metastases, impending pathologic fracture, or cord compression [NE-I 1 of 2]
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, local pain, possible vertebral compression fracture

approaches.name: Liver-directed EBRT (SBRT/SABR)
approaches.indication: Limited (<5) liver metastases. May be used alone or in combination with other liver-directed therapies [NE-I 1 of 2]

approaches.name: RT ± concurrent fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy for locally advanced unresectable disease
approaches.indication: Locally advanced unresectable disease in GI NETs and PanNETs (excluding small bowel mesenteric); locally advanced unresectable lung/thymic NETs [NET-10, NET-11, PanNET-13]

approaches.name: RT ± concurrent Cisplatin/Etoposide or Carboplatin/Etoposide
approaches.indication: Locoregional unresectable intermediate-grade (atypical carcinoid) lung/thymic NETs. Thought to have greatest efficacy in tumors with atypical histology or higher Ki-67 [NET-11]

approaches.name: Definitive chemoradiation for NEC
approaches.indication: Resectable extrapulmonary poorly differentiated NECs when definitive chemoradiation is selected instead of surgery [PDNEC-1]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for neuroendocrine tumors encompasses somatostatin analogs, molecularly targeted therapies (everolimus, sunitinib, cabozantinib), peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT), cytotoxic chemotherapy, and immunotherapy. Treatment selection is guided by tumor site of origin, histologic grade, SSTR status, Ki-67 index, disease burden, disease progression, performance status, and prior treatment history. There is no known role for systemic treatment in the adjuvant setting for NETs [NE-G 1 of 11]. Currently, there are no data to support a specific sequence of regional versus systemic therapy, and no data to guide sequencing of systemic therapy options [NE-G 1 of 11]. Doses and schedules are subject to appropriate modifications depending on the circumstances.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: GI NETs (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2) - Locoregional Advanced and/or Distant Metastases
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cabozantinib (category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus or Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate) [1]; Everolimus (category 1 for nonfunctional tumors) [2,3]; PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive and progression on Octreotide/Lanreotide, category 1 for progressive mid-gut tumors) [NE-J]; First-line PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive, Ki-67 ≥10%, clinically significant tumor burden) [4]; Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms) [5-8]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Cabozantinib showed significant PFS improvement in CABINET trial (extrapancreatic: median PFS 8.4 vs 3.9 months; HR 0.38, P < .001) [1]. Everolimus showed PFS benefit in RADIANT-4 (median PFS 11.0 vs 3.9 months; HR 0.48, P < .001) [2]. PRRT demonstrated significant PFS improvement in NETTER-1 (not reached vs 8.4 months; P < .0001) [5]. Octreotide LAR showed antiproliferative effect in PROMID trial [6]. Lanreotide showed PFS benefit in CLARINET trial (HR 0.47, P < .001) [7].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Above-label dose Octreotide LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive); cytotoxic chemotherapy (Capecitabine, Fluorouracil, Oxaliplatin-based therapy [CAPEOX or FOLFOX], Temozolomide) if no other options [NE-G 1 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: SSTR-positive status for SSA and PRRT efficacy; Ki-67 index for grade assessment and PRRT eligibility (Ki-67 ≥10% for first-line PRRT)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Lung/Thymic NETs - Distant Metastases (low grade/typical carcinoid or intermediate grade/atypical carcinoid)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cabozantinib (category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus) [1]; Everolimus (category 1 for nonfunctional lung NETs) [2,3]; Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms) [6-8]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Cabozantinib demonstrated PFS benefit in CABINET trial (extrapancreatic subgroup: HR 0.17) [1]. Everolimus showed PFS benefit in RADIANT-4 lung subgroup (HR 0.50) [2]. Lanreotide showed activity in SPINET trial [11].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin/Etoposide, Capecitabine/Temozolomide, Cisplatin/Etoposide, Oxaliplatin-based therapy, Temozolomide (Other Recommended). PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive and progression on SSA) (Useful in Certain Circumstances) [NE-G 2 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: SSTR-positive for SSA and PRRT; Ki-67 for grade distinction

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Pancreatic NETs (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2) - Locoregional Advanced and/or Distant Metastases
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cabozantinib (category 1 if prior treatment with Everolimus, Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate, or Sunitinib) [1]; Everolimus 10 mg PO daily (category 1 for progressive disease) [19]; Sunitinib 37.5 mg PO daily (category 1 for progressive disease) [20]; Capecitabine/Temozolomide (preferred when tumor response needed for symptoms or cytoreduction) [21]; Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive) [5-8]; First-line PRRT (if SSTR-positive, Ki-67 ≥10%, clinically significant tumor burden) [4]; PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive and progression on SSA) [NE-J]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Cabozantinib showed PFS benefit in CABINET pancreatic cohort (median PFS 13.8 vs 4.4 months; HR 0.23, P < .001) [1]. Everolimus showed PFS benefit in RADIANT-3 (median PFS 11.0 vs 4.6 months; P < .001) [19]. Sunitinib showed PFS benefit (median PFS 12.6 vs 5.8 months; HR 0.32, P < .000015) [20]. Capecitabine/Temozolomide showed PFS benefit vs temozolomide alone in E2211 (median PFS 22.7 vs 14.4 months; HR 0.58, P = .022) [21].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAPEOX or FOLFOX (bulky, symptomatic, progressive disease); Above-label dose SSA; SSA for SSTR-negative tumors; Belzutifan for germline VHL alteration; RT ± concurrent chemotherapy for locally advanced [NE-G 3 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: SSTR-positive for SSA and PRRT; Ki-67 index; VHL germline mutation status for belzutifan eligibility

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Well-Differentiated Grade 3 NETs - Favorable Biology (Ki-67 <55%, slow growing, SSTR-positive)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial; Cabozantinib; Capecitabine/Temozolomide; PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive); Temozolomide [NE-G 4 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Multiple treatment options available; limited prospective data guiding sequencing. Temozolomide-based therapy may have more activity in pancreas compared to GI NETs. Cabozantinib showed activity in CABINET G3 subgroup [1].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAPEOX, Everolimus, FOLFOX, Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive), Carboplatin/Etoposide, Cisplatin/Etoposide, Pembrolizumab (if MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H), Sunitinib (pancreas only) [NE-G 4 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Ki-67 <55% for favorable biology; SSTR-positive for SSA/PRRT; MSI-H/dMMR/TMB-H for pembrolizumab

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Well-Differentiated Grade 3 NETs - Unfavorable Biology (Ki-67 ≥55%, rapid growth, SSTR-negative)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial; Carboplatin/Etoposide; Cisplatin/Etoposide; Oxaliplatin-based therapy (eg, CAPEOX, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX) [NE-G 5 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Higher grade tumors more likely to respond to platinum-based chemotherapy. Data suggest platinum-based chemotherapy most active with Ki-67 ≥55% [51].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/Temozolomide, Irinotecan-based therapy (Cisplatin/Irinotecan, FOLFIRI), Temozolomide, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (category 2B), Pembrolizumab (if MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H) [NE-G 5 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Ki-67 ≥55% for unfavorable biology; MSI-H/dMMR/TMB-H for pembrolizumab

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Extrapulmonary PDNEC/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/MiNEN - Resectable
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based chemotherapy is standard of care, paralleling small cell lung cancer paradigm. Response rates and outcomes data support platinum/etoposide as first-line [393].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specific; molecular profiling recommended to identify actionable alterations

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based combinations remain standard first-line therapy.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/Temozolomide, Carboplatin/Irinotecan, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX, Temozolomide, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (metastatic with progression, category 2B). Targeted therapy based on molecular alterations: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (BRAF V600E), Entrectinib/Larotrectinib/Repotrectinib (NTRK fusions), Selpercatinib (RET fusions). Pembrolizumab (MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H) [NE-G 6 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E mutations, MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H for targeted/immunotherapy selection

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Limited treatment options for advanced PCC/PGL; clinical trials preferred.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Belzutifan (FDA approved 2024, ORR 26%) [44]; Cabozantinib (ORR 25%) [45]; Sunitinib 37.5 mg daily [46]; Systemic chemotherapy (CVD or Temozolomide); PRRT with Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (if SSTR-positive); SSAs (if SSTR-positive); Selpercatinib (if RET gene fusion-positive) [NE-G 7 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: SSTR-positive for SSA/PRRT; MIBG scan positive for MIBG therapy; RET fusions for selpercatinib; VHL/SDH gene status

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin/Etoposide ± Doxorubicin ± Mitotane; Cisplatin/Etoposide ± Doxorubicin ± Mitotane [NE-G 8 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based combination with mitotane is standard. FIRM-ACT trial showed improved response rates (23.2% vs 9.2%) and PFS (5.0 vs 2.1 months) with EDP-M vs streptozocin-M [50].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib [51]; Mitotane monotherapy; Mitotane + Pembrolizumab [52,53]; Pembrolizumab [52,53]; Ipilimumab + Nivolumab; Osilodrostat (for symptom control) [NE-G 8 of 11]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Monitor Mitotane blood levels (target 14–20 mcg/mL). Life-long hydrocortisone ± fludrocortisone replacement required.

key_regimens.name: Octreotide acetate LAR
key_regimens.setting: GI NETs, PanNETs, Lung/Thymic NETs (SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Injection site reactions, diarrhea, nausea, biliary complications (gallstones), hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia

key_regimens.name: Lanreotide
key_regimens.setting: GI NETs, PanNETs, Lung/Thymic NETs (SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Injection site reactions, diarrhea, nausea, biliary complications, hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia

key_regimens.name: Telotristat (for carcinoid syndrome diarrhea)
key_regimens.setting: Carcinoid syndrome with refractory diarrhea (add-on to SSA)

key_regimens.name: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (PRRT)
key_regimens.setting: SSTR-positive GEP-NETs, G3 NETs with favorable biology
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea/vomiting (amino acid infusion-related), myelosuppression, renal toxicity, hepatic toxicity (high liver burden), secondary MDS/leukemia (rare), bowel obstruction/ischemia (rare)

key_regimens.name: Everolimus
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Stomatitis, rash, fatigue, diarrhea, hyperglycemia, pneumonitis (rare), hypertriglyceridemia (rare pancreatitis)

key_regimens.name: Sunitinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, diarrhea, mucositis, hypertension, hand-foot syndrome, congestive heart failure (rare)

key_regimens.name: Cabozantinib
key_regimens.setting: Progressive NETs (GI, lung, PanNETs) after prior therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, diarrhea, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, weight loss, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine/Temozolomide (CAPTEM)
key_regimens.setting: Advanced PanNETs (preferred when tumor response needed); G3 NETs

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide
key_regimens.setting: Extrapulmonary PDNEC, advanced lung/thymic NETs (intermediate grade)

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide
key_regimens.setting: Extrapulmonary PDNEC, advanced lung/thymic NETs (intermediate grade)

key_regimens.name: EDP-Mitotane (Etoposide/Doxorubicin/Cisplatin ± Mitotane)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, GI toxicity, adrenal insufficiency (mitotane), neurological effects (mitotane), hepatotoxicity

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab/Nivolumab (for advanced NETs)
key_regimens.setting: Unfavorable biology G3 NETs (category 2B), metastatic PDNEC (after progression)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, rash)

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors after progression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, rash)

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For resected NETs: 12 weeks to 12 months post-resection (earlier if symptomatic), then every 12–24 months for up to 10 years. For advanced/metastatic disease: every 12 weeks to 12 months based on clinical or pathologic signs of aggressiveness. SSTR-based imaging should be repeated at time of progressive disease on cross-sectional imaging [NET-8, PanNET-11, NE-B 1 of 5]
response_logic: Multiphasic (dual-phase) contrast-enhanced CT is important for monitoring liver metastases as NETs frequently enhance in arterial phase [NE-B 1 of 5]
SSTR-PET/CT should be repeated at the time of progressive disease on cross-sectional imaging to reassess SSTR status [NET-8]
Consider same imaging modality and contrast agent for follow-up studies for consistent imaging interpretation [NE-B 1 of 5]
For functional tumors, biochemical markers (5-HIAA for carcinoid syndrome, specific hormone markers) should be followed as clinically indicated [NET-8]
Chromogranin A monitoring is not routinely recommended due to challenges with reproducibility and interference from PPIs and comorbidities [NE-A 1 of 4]

For carcinoid syndrome, echocardiogram should be performed every 1–3 years or as clinically indicated for patients without CHD, and at least annually for patients with established CHD [NET-14, NE-B 2 of 5]
For resected ACC: Every 12 wk–12 mo up to 10 y (after 10 y as clinically indicated) with chest CT ± contrast and abdomen CT or MRI with contrast, and biomarkers if tumor initially functional [AGT-5]
For resected PCC/PGL: H&P, blood pressure, and markers annually or sooner if symptomatic, for up to 10 years [PHEO-3]
After 10 years post-resection, surveillance should be considered as clinically indicated. Data are limited on optimal surveillance beyond 10 years [NET-8, NE-B 2 of 5]
imaging_recommendations: Primary GI NETs: Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI, chest CT ± contrast as clinically indicated [NET-8]
Primary lung/thymic NETs: Chest CT + multiphasic CT or MRI abdomen with contrast, pelvis CT or MRI with contrast as clinically indicated [NET-8]
SSTR-based imaging and FDG-PET/CT are not recommended for routine surveillance [NET-8, PanNET-11]
For metastatic disease monitoring: consider same imaging modality for consistent interpretation [NE-B 1 of 5]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: In select cases, resection may be considered for recurrent locoregional disease, isolated distant metastases, or previously unresectable tumor that has regressed [NET-8]
For patients with disease progression on SSA therapy, consider switching to alternative first-line systemic therapy [NET-12, PanNET-13]
Consider enrollment in clinical trials at time of disease progression
For suspected carcinoid heart disease, cardiology consultation recommended [NET-14]
